**Munich**[[a]](#cite_note-3) ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *München* [[ˈmʏnçn̩]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Muenchentitle.ogg "File:Muenchentitle.ogg"))[[b]](#cite_note-4) is the capital and most populous city of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), Germany. As of 30 November 2024, its population was 1,604,384,[[3]](#cite_note-stadtbevoelkerung-5) making it the third-largest city in Germany after [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") and [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg"). Munich is the largest city in Germany that is not [a state](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") of [its own](/wiki/City-state "City-state"), and it ranks as the 11th-largest city in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU). The metropolitan area has around 3 million inhabitants, and the broader [Munich Metropolitan Region](/wiki/Munich_Metropolitan_Region "Munich Metropolitan Region") is home to about 6.2 million people.[[4]](#cite_note-mmr_official_site-6) It is the [third largest metropolitan region by GDP in the EU](/wiki/List_of_EU_metropolitan_regions_by_GDP#2021_ranking_of_top_four_German_metropolitan_regions "List of EU metropolitan regions by GDP"). Munich is located on the river [Isar](/wiki/Isar "Isar") north of the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"). It is the seat of the [Upper Bavarian](/wiki/Upper_Bavaria "Upper Bavaria") administrative region. With 4,500 people per km2, Munich is Germany's most densely populated municipality. It is also the second-largest city in the [Bavarian dialect area](/wiki/Bavarian_language "Bavarian language") after [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna").

The first record of Munich dates to 1158. The city has played an important role in Bavarian and German history. During the [Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), it remained a Catholic stronghold. Munich became the capital of the [Kingdom of Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria") in 1806 and developed as a centre for arts, architecture, culture, and science. The [House of Wittelsbach](/wiki/House_of_Wittelsbach "House of Wittelsbach") ruled until 1918, when the [German revolution of 1918–1919](/wiki/German_revolution_of_1918%E2%80%931919 "German revolution of 1918–1919") ended their reign and saw the short-lived [Bavarian Soviet Republic](/wiki/Bavarian_Soviet_Republic "Bavarian Soviet Republic"). In the 1920s, Munich became a centre of political movements, including the rise of the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party"). The city was known as the "Capital of the Movement". During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Munich was heavily bombed, but much of its historic architecture has since been restored.[[5]](#cite_note-englund-7)[[6]](#cite_note-zensus-8) After the war, the city's population and economy grew rapidly. Munich hosted the [1972 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics") and the [1974 FIFA World Cup final](/wiki/1974_FIFA_World_Cup_final "1974 FIFA World Cup final").

Munich is a major centre for science, technology, finance, innovation, business, and tourism. It has a high standard of living, ranking first in Germany and third worldwide in the 2018 [Mercer](/wiki/Mercer_(consulting_firm) "Mercer (consulting firm)") survey.[[7]](#cite_note-mercer-9) It was named the world's most liveable city by [*Monocle*](/wiki/Monocle_(brand) "Monocle (brand)")'s [Quality of Life Survey](/wiki/City_quality_of_life_indices "City quality of life indices") 2018.[[8]](#cite_note-forbes-10) Munich is the wealthiest city in the EU by GDP per capita among cities with over one million inhabitants[[9]](#cite_note-11) and is among the most expensive German cities for real estate and rents.[[10]](#cite_note-biimmobilien-12)[[11]](#cite_note-hausimmobilien-13) In 2023, 30.1% of residents were foreigners, and 19.4% were German citizens with a [migration background](/wiki/Migration_background "Migration background") from abroad.[[12]](#cite_note-migr2023-14) Munich's economy is based on [high tech](/wiki/High_tech "High tech"), [automobiles](/wiki/Automobiles "Automobiles"), the [service sector](/wiki/Service_sector "Service sector"), [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology"), [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), engineering, and [electronics](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics"). Multinational companies such as [BMW](/wiki/BMW "BMW"), [Siemens](/wiki/Siemens "Siemens"), [Allianz SE](/wiki/Allianz_SE "Allianz SE"), and [Munich Re](/wiki/Munich_Re "Munich Re") are headquartered there. The city has two research universities and many scientific institutions.[[13]](#cite_note-nature-15) Munich is known for its architecture, cultural venues, sports events, exhibitions, and the annual [Oktoberfest](/wiki/Oktoberfest "Oktoberfest"), the world's largest *[Volksfest](/wiki/Volksfest "Volksfest")*.[[14]](#cite_note-tourismus-16)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Munich](/wiki/History_of_Munich "History of Munich")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Munich](/wiki/Timeline_of_Munich "Timeline of Munich").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/DEU_M%C3%BCnchen_gross_COA.svg/250px-DEU_M%C3%BCnchen_gross_COA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:DEU_M%C3%BCnchen_gross_COA.svg)

[Coat of arms of Munich](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Munich "Coat of arms of Munich")



The unofficial city anthem of Munich, recorded in 1929

### Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Etymology")]

Munich was a tiny 12th-century [monastic](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism") settlement, which was named *zu den Munchen* ("to the monks"). The Old High German *Munche* served as the base for the modern German city name *München*.[[15]](#cite_note-17)

### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

The river [Isar](/wiki/Isar "Isar") was a prehistoric [trade route](/wiki/Trade_route "Trade route") and in the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") Munich was among the largest [raft ports](/wiki/Raft "Raft") in Europe.[[16]](#cite_note-Bernan_Associates_UNESCO-18) Bronze Age settlements up to four millennia old have been discovered.[[17]](#cite_note-19) Evidence of [Celtic](/wiki/Celt "Celt") settlements from the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") have been discovered in areas around [Ramersdorf-Perlach](/wiki/Ramersdorf-Perlach "Ramersdorf-Perlach").[[18]](#cite_note-20)

### Roman period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Roman period")]

The ancient Roman road Via Julia, which connected [Augsburg](/wiki/Augsburg "Augsburg") and [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg"), crossed over the Isar south of Munich, at the towns of [Baierbrunn](/wiki/Baierbrunn "Baierbrunn") and [Gauting](/wiki/Gauting "Gauting").[[19]](#cite_note-21) A Roman settlement north-east of Munich was excavated in the neighborhood of [Denning](/wiki/Denning_(Munich) "Denning (Munich)").[[20]](#cite_note-22)

### Post-Roman settlements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Post-Roman settlements")]

Starting in the 6th century, the [Baiuvarii](/wiki/Baiuvarii "Baiuvarii") populated the area around what is now modern Munich, such as in Johanneskirchen, Feldmoching, Bogenhausen and Pasing.[[21]](#cite_note-23)[[22]](#cite_note-24) The first known Christian church was built ca. 815 in Fröttmanning.[[23]](#cite_note-25)

### Origin of medieval town

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Origin of medieval town")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Stadtansicht_1572.jpg/250px-Stadtansicht_1572.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stadtansicht_1572.jpg)

Munich in the 16th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Muenchen_merian.jpg/250px-Muenchen_merian.jpg)](/wiki/File:Muenchen_merian.jpg)

Plan of Munich in 1642

The first medieval bridges across the river Isar were located in current city areas of Munich and [Landshut](/wiki/Landshut "Landshut").[[16]](#cite_note-Bernan_Associates_UNESCO-18) [Henry the Lion](/wiki/Henry_the_Lion "Henry the Lion"), the Duke of Saxony and Bavaria, founded the town of Munich in his territory to control the [salt trade](/wiki/Salt_trade "Salt trade"), after having burned down the town of Föhring and its bridges over the Isar.[[24]](#cite_note-26) Historians date this event at about 1158.[[25]](#cite_note-freed-27): 167 The layout of Munich city, with five city gates and market place, resembled that of [Höxter](/wiki/H%C3%B6xter "Höxter").[[25]](#cite_note-freed-27): 168

Henry built a new toll bridge, customs house and a coin market closer to his home, somewhat upstream, at a settlement around the area of modern old town Munich. This new toll bridge most likely crossed the Isar where the Museuminsel and the modern Ludwigsbrücke are now located.[[26]](#cite_note-28)

[Otto of Freising](/wiki/Otto_of_Freising "Otto of Freising") protested to his nephew, [Emperor Frederick Barbarossa](/wiki/Frederick_Barbarossa "Frederick Barbarossa"). However, on 14 June 1158, in [Augsburg](/wiki/Augsburg "Augsburg"), the conflict was settled in favor of Duke Henry. The *[Augsburg Arbitration](/wiki/Augsburg_Arbitration "Augsburg Arbitration")* mentions the name of the location in dispute as *forum apud Munichen*. Although Bishop Otto had lost his bridge, the arbiters ordered Duke Henry to pay a third of his income to the Bishop in Freising as compensation.[[27]](#cite_note-29)[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-31)

14 June 1158 is considered the official founding day of the city of Munich. Archaeological excavations at Marienhof Square (near [Marienplatz](/wiki/Marienplatz "Marienplatz")) in advance of the expansion of the S-Bahn (subway) in 2012 discovered shards of vessels from the 11th century, which prove again that the settlement of Munich must be older than the Augsburg Arbitration of 1158.[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33) The old [St. Peter's Church](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Church,_Munich "St. Peter's Church, Munich") near Marienplatz is also believed to predate the founding date of the town.[[32]](#cite_note-34)

In 1175, Munich received city status and fortification. In 1180, after Henry the Lion's fall from grace with Emperor Frederick Barbarossa, including his trial and exile, [Otto I Wittelsbach](/wiki/Otto_I_of_Wittelsbach,_Duke_of_Bavaria "Otto I of Wittelsbach, Duke of Bavaria") became Duke of Bavaria, and Munich was handed to the [Bishop of Freising](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Munich_and_Freising "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Munich and Freising"). In 1240, Munich was transferred to [Otto II Wittelsbach](/wiki/Otto_II_Wittelsbach,_Duke_of_Bavaria "Otto II Wittelsbach, Duke of Bavaria") and in 1255, when the [Duchy of Bavaria](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bavaria "Duchy of Bavaria") was split in two, Munich became the ducal residence of [Upper Bavaria](/wiki/Upper_Bavaria "Upper Bavaria").

Duke [Louis IV](/wiki/Louis_IV,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Louis IV, Holy Roman Emperor"), a native of Munich, was elected German king in 1314 and crowned as [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") in 1328. He strengthened the city's position by granting it the salt monopoly, thus assuring it of additional income.

On 13 February 1327, a large fire broke out in Munich that lasted two days and destroyed about a third of the town.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

In 1349, the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") ravaged Munich and Bavaria.[[34]](#cite_note-36)

The growth of Munich was supplemented by its location on top of a gravel bed, where the Isar branched into [Munich City Streams](/wiki/Munich_City_Streams "Munich City Streams"), which in turn provided power for many mills and industries within Munich.

In the 15th century, Munich underwent a revival of [Gothic arts](/wiki/Gothic_art "Gothic art"): the Old Town Hall was enlarged, and Munich's largest [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") church – the [Frauenkirche](/wiki/Munich_Frauenkirche "Munich Frauenkirche") – now a cathedral, was constructed in only 20 years, starting in 1468.

### Capital of reunited Bavaria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Capital of reunited Bavaria")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Antiquarium%2C_M%C3%BCnchner_Residenz.jpg/330px-Antiquarium%2C_M%C3%BCnchner_Residenz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Antiquarium,_M%C3%BCnchner_Residenz.jpg)

The Renaissance *Antiquarium* of the Residenz

When Bavaria was reunited in 1506 after a brief [war](/wiki/War_of_the_Succession_of_Landshut "War of the Succession of Landshut") against the Duchy of [Landshut](/wiki/Bavaria-Landshut "Bavaria-Landshut"), Munich became its capital. The arts and politics became increasingly influenced by the court.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") movement beset Munich and the Bavarian branch of the [House of Wittelsbach](/wiki/House_of_Wittelsbach "House of Wittelsbach") under Duke [Albrecht V](/wiki/Albert_V,_Duke_of_Bavaria "Albert V, Duke of Bavaria") who bolstered their prestige by conjuring up a lineage that reached back to [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity"). In 1568 Albrecht V built the Antiquarium to house the [Wittelsbach collection of Greek and Roman antiquities](/wiki/Staatliche_Antikensammlungen "Staatliche Antikensammlungen") in the [Munich Residenz](/wiki/Residenz,_Munich "Residenz, Munich").[[35]](#cite_note-37) Albrecht V appointed the composer [Orlando di Lasso](/wiki/Orlando_di_Lasso "Orlando di Lasso") as director of the court orchestra and tempted numerous Italian musicians to work at the Munich court, establishing Munich as a hub for late [Renaissance music](/wiki/Renaissance_music "Renaissance music").[[36]](#cite_note-38) During the rule of [Duke William V](/wiki/William_V,_Duke_of_Bavaria "William V, Duke of Bavaria") Munich began to be called the "German Rome" and William V began presenting Emperor [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") as ancestor of the Wittelsbach dynasty.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

Duke William V further cemented the Wittelsbach rule by commissioning the [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") [Michaelskirche](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_Church,_Munich "St. Michael's Church, Munich"). He had the sermons of his Jesuit court preacher [Jeremias Drexel](/wiki/Jeremias_Drexel "Jeremias Drexel") translated from Latin into German and published them to a greater audience.[[38]](#cite_note-40) William V was addressed with the epithet "the Pious" and like his contemporary Wittelsbach dukes promoted himself as "father of the land" (*Landesvater*), encouraged pilgrimages and [Marian devotions](/wiki/Veneration_of_Mary_in_the_Catholic_Church "Veneration of Mary in the Catholic Church").[[39]](#cite_note-41) William V had the [Hofbräuhaus](/wiki/Hofbr%C3%A4uhaus "Hofbräuhaus") built in 1589. It would become the prototype for [beer halls](/wiki/Beer_hall "Beer hall") across Munich. After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") the Residenze, the Hofbräuhaus, the [Frauenkirche](/wiki/Munich_Frauenkirche "Munich Frauenkirche"), and the [Peterskirche](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Church,_Munich "St. Peter's Church, Munich") were reconstructed to look exactly as they did before the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") seized power in 1933.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Marcktzumuenchen.png/250px-Marcktzumuenchen.png)](/wiki/File:Marcktzumuenchen.png)

[Marienplatz](/wiki/Marienplatz "Marienplatz"), Munich, about 1650

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Mun_flags_frauenkirche.jpg/250px-Mun_flags_frauenkirche.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mun_flags_frauenkirche.jpg)

Banners with the colours of Munich (left) and Bavaria (right) with the [Frauenkirche](/wiki/Munich_Frauenkirche "Munich Frauenkirche") in the background

The [Catholic League](/wiki/Catholic_League_(German) "Catholic League (German)") was founded in Munich in 1609. In 1623, during the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War") (1618–1648), Munich became an electoral residence when Duke [Maximilian I](/wiki/Maximilian_I,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Maximilian I, Elector of Bavaria") was invested with the [electoral dignity](/wiki/Prince-elector "Prince-elector"), but in 1632 the city was occupied by King [Gustavus Adolphus of Sweden](/wiki/Gustavus_Adolphus_of_Sweden "Gustavus Adolphus of Sweden").[[41]](#cite_note-43)

In 1634 Swedish and Spanish troops advanced on Munich. Maximilian I published a plague ordinance to halt an epidemic escalation.[[42]](#cite_note-44) The [bubonic plague](/wiki/Bubonic_plague "Bubonic plague") nevertheless ravaged Munich and the surrounding countryside in 1634 and 1635.[[43]](#cite_note-45) During the Thirty Years' War (1618–1648) troops again converged on Munich in 1647 and precautions were taken, so as to avoid another epidemic.[[44]](#cite_note-46)

Under the regency of the Bavarian electors, Munich was an important centre of [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") life, but also had to suffer under [Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") occupations in 1704 and 1742.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] When Elector [Maximilian III Joseph](/wiki/Maximilian_III_Joseph "Maximilian III Joseph") died in 1777, the succession empowered the [Palatinate](/wiki/Count_palatine "Count palatine") branch within the [House of Wittelsbach](/wiki/House_of_Wittelsbach "House of Wittelsbach").[[45]](#cite_note-47)

In 1777 Bavarian lands were inherited by [Karl Theodor](/wiki/Charles_Theodore,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Charles Theodore, Elector of Bavaria"). The new duke was disliked by the citizens of Munich for his supposedly enlightened ideas. In 1785 Karl Theodor invited Count Rumford [Benjamin Thompson](/wiki/Benjamin_Thompson "Benjamin Thompson") to take up residency in Munich and implement stringent social reforms. The poor were forced to live in newly built [workhouses](/wiki/Workhouses "Workhouses"). The Bavarian army was restructured, with common soldiers receiving better food and reassurances that they would be treated humanely by officers.[[46]](#cite_note-48) Munich was the largest German city to lose [fortification](/wiki/Fortification "Fortification") in the 1790s.[[47]](#cite_note-49) In 1791 Karl Theodor and Count Rumford started to demolish Munich's fortifications.[[48]](#cite_note-50) After 1793 Munich's citizens, including house servants, carpenters, butchers, merchants, and court officials, seized the opportunity, building new houses, stalls, and sheds outside the city walls.[[49]](#cite_note-51)

After making an alliance with Napoleonic France, the city became the capital of the new [Kingdom of Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria") in 1806 with Elector [Maximillian IV Joseph](/w/index.php?title=Maximillian_IV_Joseph&action=edit&redlink=1 "Maximillian IV Joseph (page does not exist)") becoming its first king. The state parliament (the *[Landtag](/wiki/Landtag "Landtag")*) and the new [archdiocese of Munich and Freising](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Munich_and_Freising "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Munich and Freising") were also located in the city.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

The establishment of Bavarian state sovereignty profoundly affected Munich. Munich became the center of a modernizing kingdom, and one of the king's first acts was the [secularization](/wiki/Secularization "Secularization") of Bavaria. He had dissolved all [monasteries](/wiki/Monasteries "Monasteries") in 1802 and once crowned, Maximilian Joseph generated state revenues by selling off church lands. While many monasteries were reestablished, Maximilian Joseph I succeeded in controlling the right to brew beer (*Braurecht*). The king handed the brewing monopoly to Munich's wealthiest brewers, who in turn paid substantial taxes on their beer production. In 1807 the king abolished all ordinances that limited the number of apprentices and [journeymen](/wiki/Journeymen "Journeymen") a brewery could employ. Munich's population had swelled and Munich brewers were now free to employ as many workers as they needed to meet the demand.[[51]](#cite_note-53) In October 1810 a beer festival was held on the meadows just outside Munich to commemorate the wedding of the crown prince and princess [Therese of Saxe-Hildburghausen](/wiki/Therese_of_Saxe-Hildburghausen "Therese of Saxe-Hildburghausen"). The parades in regional dress (*Tracht*) represented the diversity of the kingdom. The fields are now part of the [Theresienwiese](/wiki/Theresienwiese "Theresienwiese") and the celebrations developed into Munich's annual [Oktoberfest](/wiki/Oktoberfest "Oktoberfest").[[52]](#cite_note-54)

The Bavarian state proceeded to take control over the beer market, by regulating all taxes on beer in 1806 and 1811. Brewers and the beer taverns (*Wirtshäuser*) were taxed, and the state also controlled the quality of beer while limiting the [competition](/wiki/Competition_(economics) "Competition (economics)") among breweries.[[53]](#cite_note-55) In 1831 the king's government introduced a cost-of-living allowance on beer for lower-ranking civil servants and soldiers. Soldiers stationed in Munich were granted a daily allowance for beer in the early 1840s.[[54]](#cite_note-56) By the 1850s beer had become essential [staple food](/wiki/Staple_food "Staple food") for Munich's working and lower classes. Since the Middle Ages beer had been regarded as nutritious *liquid bread* (*fließendes Brot*) in Bavaria. But Munich suffered from poor [water sanitation](/wiki/Sanitation "Sanitation") and as early as the 1700s beer came to be regarded as the *fifth element*. Beer was essential in maintaining [public health](/wiki/Public_health "Public health") in Munich and in the mid-1840s Munich police estimated that at least 40,000 residents relied primarily on beer for their nutrition.[[55]](#cite_note-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Justizpalast_Munich.jpg/250px-Justizpalast_Munich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Justizpalast_Munich.jpg)

The [Palace of Justice](/wiki/Justizpalast_(Munich) "Justizpalast (Munich)") in Baroque Revival style

In 1832 [Peter von Hess](/wiki/Peter_von_Hess "Peter von Hess") painted the [Greek War of Independence](/wiki/Greek_War_of_Independence "Greek War of Independence") at the order of King [Ludwig I](/wiki/Ludwig_I_of_Bavaria "Ludwig I of Bavaria"). Ludwig I had the [Königsplatz](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsplatz,_Munich "Königsplatz, Munich") built in neoclassicism as a matter of ideological choice. [Leo von Klenze](/wiki/Leo_von_Klenze "Leo von Klenze") supervised the construction of a [Propylaea](/wiki/Propylaea_(Munich) "Propylaea (Munich)") between 1854 and 1862.[[56]](#cite_note-58)

During the early to mid-19th century, the old fortified city walls of Munich were largely demolished due to population expansion.[[57]](#cite_note-59) The first Munich railway station was built in 1839, with a line going to [Augsburg](/wiki/Augsburg "Augsburg") in the west. By 1849 a newer Munich Central Train Station ([München Hauptbahnhof](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchen_Hauptbahnhof "München Hauptbahnhof")) was completed, with a line going to [Landshut](/wiki/Landshut "Landshut") and [Regensburg](/wiki/Regensburg "Regensburg") in the north.[[58]](#cite_note-60)[[59]](#cite_note-61) In 1825 [Ludwig I](/wiki/Ludwig_I_of_Bavaria "Ludwig I of Bavaria") had ascended to the throne and commissioned leading architects such as [Leo von Klenze](/wiki/Leo_von_Klenze "Leo von Klenze") to design a series of public museums in [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") style. The grand building projects of Ludwig I gave Munich the endearment "Isar-Athen" and "Monaco di Bavaria".[[60]](#cite_note-62) Between 1856 and 1861 the court gardener [Carl von Effner](/wiki/Carl_von_Effner "Carl von Effner") landscaped the banks of the river [Isar](/wiki/Isar "Isar") and established the *Maximilian Gardens*. From 1848 the *[Münchner Neueste Nachrichten](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchner_Neueste_Nachrichten "Münchner Neueste Nachrichten")* was published as a regional newspaper in Munich. In 1857 the construction of the [Maximilianeum](/wiki/Maximilianeum "Maximilianeum") was begun.[[61]](#cite_note-63)

By the time [Ludwig II](/wiki/Ludwig_II_of_Bavaria "Ludwig II of Bavaria") became king in 1864, he remained mostly aloof from his capital and focused more on his fanciful castles in the Bavarian countryside, which is why he is known the world over as the 'fairytale king'. Ludwig II tried to lure [Richard Wagner](/wiki/Richard_Wagner "Richard Wagner") to Munich, but his plans for an opera house were declined by the city council. Ludwig II nevertheless generated a windfall for Munich's craft and construction industries. In 1876 Munich hosted the first German Art and Industry Exhibition, which showcased the northern [Neo-Renaissance](/wiki/Neo-Renaissance "Neo-Renaissance") fashion that came to be the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire")'s predominant style. Munich based artists put on the German National Applied Arts Exhibition in 1888, showcasing [Baroque Revival architecture](/wiki/Baroque_Revival_architecture "Baroque Revival architecture") and [Rococo Revival](/wiki/Rococo_Revival "Rococo Revival") designs.[[62]](#cite_note-64)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/M%C3%BCnchen_Jugendstil_1.jpg/250px-M%C3%BCnchen_Jugendstil_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:M%C3%BCnchen_Jugendstil_1.jpg)

Jugendstil style house at Leopoldstr. 77, [Münchner Freiheit](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchner_Freiheit "Münchner Freiheit")

In 1900 [Wilhelm Röntgen](/wiki/Wilhelm_R%C3%B6ntgen "Wilhelm Röntgen") moved to Munich, where he was appointed as a professor of physics. In 1901 he was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics.[[63]](#cite_note-65)

The Prince Regent [Luitpold](/wiki/Luitpold,_Prince_Regent_of_Bavaria "Luitpold, Prince Regent of Bavaria")'s reign from 1886 to 1912 was marked by tremendous artistic and cultural activity in Munich.[[64]](#cite_note-66) At the dawn of the 20th century Munich was an epicenter for the [Jugendstil](/wiki/Jugendstil "Jugendstil") movement, combining a liberal magazine culture with progressive [industrial design](/wiki/Industrial_design "Industrial design") and architecture. The German art movement took its name from the Munich magazine *Die Jugend* (*The Youth*).[[65]](#cite_note-67) Prominent Munich Jugendstil artists include [Hans Eduard von Berlepsch-Valendas](/wiki/Hans_Eduard_von_Berlepsch-Valendas "Hans Eduard von Berlepsch-Valendas"), [Otto Eckmann](/wiki/Otto_Eckmann "Otto Eckmann"),[[66]](#cite_note-68) [Margarethe von Brauchitsch](/w/index.php?title=Margarethe_von_Brauchitsch&action=edit&redlink=1 "Margarethe von Brauchitsch (page does not exist)"), [August Endell](/wiki/August_Endell "August Endell"), [Hermann Obrist](/wiki/Hermann_Obrist "Hermann Obrist"), [Wilhelm von Debschitz](/wiki/Wilhelm_von_Debschitz "Wilhelm von Debschitz"),[[67]](#cite_note-69) and [Richard Riemerschmid](/wiki/Richard_Riemerschmid "Richard Riemerschmid"). In 1905 two large department stores opened in Munich, the Kaufhaus Oberpollinger and the [Warenhaus Hermann Tietz](/wiki/Hermann_Tietz "Hermann Tietz"), both having been designed by the architect [Max Littmann](/wiki/Max_Littmann "Max Littmann").[[68]](#cite_note-70) In 1911 the [expressionist](/wiki/Expressionist "Expressionist") group [Der Blaue Reiter](/wiki/Der_Blaue_Reiter "Der Blaue Reiter") was established in Munich. Its founding members include [Gabriele Münter](/wiki/Gabriele_M%C3%BCnter "Gabriele Münter").[[69]](#cite_note-71)

### World War I to World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: World War I to World War II")]

Following the outbreak of World War I in 1914, life in Munich became very difficult, as the [Allied blockade of Germany](/wiki/Blockade_of_Germany_(1914%E2%80%931919) "Blockade of Germany (1914–1919)") led to food and fuel shortages. During French air raids in 1916, three bombs fell on Munich.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1916, the 'Bayerische Motoren Werke' ([BMW](/wiki/BMW "BMW")) produced its first [aircraft engine](/wiki/Aircraft_engine "Aircraft engine") in Munich.[[70]](#cite_note-72) The public limited company BMW AG was founded in 1918, with [Camillo Castiglioni](/wiki/Camillo_Castiglioni "Camillo Castiglioni") owning one third of the share capital. In 1922 BMW relocated its headquarters to a factory in Munich.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

After World War I, the city was at the centre of substantial political unrest. In November 1918, on the eve of the German revolution, [Ludwig III of Bavaria](/wiki/Ludwig_III_of_Bavaria "Ludwig III of Bavaria") and his family fled the city. After the murder of the first republican [premier of Bavaria](/wiki/List_of_Ministers-President_of_Bavaria "List of Ministers-President of Bavaria") [Kurt Eisner](/wiki/Kurt_Eisner "Kurt Eisner") in February 1919 by [Anton Graf von Arco auf Valley](/wiki/Anton_Graf_von_Arco_auf_Valley "Anton Graf von Arco auf Valley"), the [Bavarian Soviet Republic](/wiki/Bavarian_Soviet_Republic "Bavarian Soviet Republic") was proclaimed.[[72]](#cite_note-74) The November 1918 revolution ended the reign of the Wittelsbach in Bavaria.[[73]](#cite_note-City_Halls_and_Civic_Materialism-75) In *[Mein Kampf](/wiki/Mein_Kampf "Mein Kampf")* [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") described his political activism in Munich after November 1918 as the "Beginning of My Political Activity". Hitler called the short-lived Bavarian Soviet Republic "the rule of the Jews".[[74]](#cite_note-76) In 1919 [Bavaria Film](/wiki/Bavaria_Film "Bavaria Film") was founded and in the 1920s Munich offered film makers an alternative to Germany's largest film studio, [Babelsberg Studio](/wiki/Babelsberg_Studio "Babelsberg Studio").[[75]](#cite_note-77)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Bundesarchiv_Bild_119-1486%2C_Hitler-Putsch%2C_M%C3%BCnchen%2C_Marienplatz.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_119-1486%2C_Hitler-Putsch%2C_M%C3%BCnchen%2C_Marienplatz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_119-1486,_Hitler-Putsch,_M%C3%BCnchen,_Marienplatz.jpg)

Unrest during the [Beer Hall Putsch](/wiki/Beer_Hall_Putsch "Beer Hall Putsch")

In 1923 [Gustav von Kahr](/wiki/Gustav_von_Kahr "Gustav von Kahr") was appointed Bavarian prime minister and immediately planned for the expulsion of all Jews who did not hold German citizenship. Chief of Police [Ernst Pöhner](/wiki/Ernst_P%C3%B6hner "Ernst Pöhner") and [Wilhelm Frick](/wiki/Wilhelm_Frick "Wilhelm Frick") openly indulged in antisemitism, while Bavarian judges praised people on the political right as patriotic for their crimes and handed down mild sentences.[[76]](#cite_note-78) In 1923, Adolf Hitler and his supporters, who were concentrated in Munich, staged the [Beer Hall Putsch](/wiki/Beer_Hall_Putsch "Beer Hall Putsch"), an attempt to overthrow the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic") and seize power. The revolt failed, resulting in Hitler's arrest and the temporary crippling of the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") (NSDAP).[[77]](#cite_note-79)

Munich was chosen as capital for the [Free State of Bavaria](/wiki/Free_State_of_Bavaria "Free State of Bavaria") and acquired increased responsibility for administering the city itself and the surrounding districts. Offices needed to be built for bureaucracy, so a 12-story office building was erected in the southern part of the historic city centre in the late 1920s.[[73]](#cite_note-City_Halls_and_Civic_Materialism-75)

Munich again became important to the Nazis when they took power in Germany in 1933. The party created its first [concentration camp](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camp "Nazi concentration camp") at [Dachau](/wiki/Dachau_concentration_camp "Dachau concentration camp"), 16 km (10 mi) north-west of the city. Because of its importance to the rise of National Socialism, Munich was referred to as the *Hauptstadt der Bewegung* ("Capital of the Movement").[[78]](#cite_note-80)

The NSDAP headquarters and the documentation apparatus for controlling all aspects of life were located in Munich. Nazi organizations, such as the [National Socialist Women's League](/wiki/National_Socialist_Women%27s_League "National Socialist Women's League") and the [Gestapo](/wiki/Gestapo "Gestapo"), had their offices along [Brienner Straße](/wiki/Brienner_Stra%C3%9Fe_(Munich) "Brienner Straße (Munich)") and around the [Königsplatz](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsplatz,_Munich "Königsplatz, Munich"). The party acquired 68 buildings in the area and many *Führerbauten* ("*Führer* buildings") were built to reflect a new aesthetic of power.[[79]](#cite_note-81) Construction work for the [Führerbau](/wiki/F%C3%BChrerbau "Führerbau") and the party headquarters (known as the [Brown House](/wiki/Brown_House,_Munich "Brown House, Munich")) started in September 1933.[[80]](#cite_note-82) The *[Haus der Kunst](/wiki/Haus_der_Kunst "Haus der Kunst")* (House of German Art) was the first building to be commissioned by Hitler. The architect [Paul Troost](/wiki/Paul_Troost "Paul Troost") was asked to start work shortly after the Nazis had seized power because "the most German of all German cities" was left with no exhibition building when in 1931 the [Glass Palace](/wiki/Glaspalast_(Munich) "Glaspalast (Munich)") was destroyed in an arson attack.[[81]](#cite_note-83) The *Red Terror* that supposedly preceded Nazi control in Munich was detailed in Nazi publications; seminal accounts are that of Rudolf Schricker *Rotmord über München* published in 1934, and *Die Blutchronik des Marxismus in Deutschland* by Adolf Ehrt and Hans Roden.[[82]](#cite_note-84)

In 1930 *Feinkost Käfer* was founded in Munich; the *Käfer* catering business is now a world leading party service.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

The city was the site where the 1938 [Munich Agreement](/wiki/Munich_Agreement "Munich Agreement") signed between the United Kingdom and the [Third French Republic](/wiki/Third_French_Republic "Third French Republic") with [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") as part of the Franco-British policy of [appeasement](/wiki/Appeasement "Appeasement"). The British Prime Minister [Neville Chamberlain](/wiki/Neville_Chamberlain "Neville Chamberlain") assented to the German annexation of [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia")'s [Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland") in the hopes of satisfying Hitler's territorial expansion.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

The [Munich-Riem Airport](/wiki/Munich-Riem_Airport "Munich-Riem Airport") was completed in October 1939.[[85]](#cite_note-87)

On 8 November 1939, shortly after the Second World War had begun, [Georg Elser](/wiki/Georg_Elser "Georg Elser") planted a bomb in the Bürgerbräukeller in Munich in an attempt to assassinate Adolf Hitler, who held a political party speech. Hitler, however, had left the building minutes before the bomb went off.[[86]](#cite_note-88) By mid 1942 the majority of Jews living in Munich and the suburbs had been deported.[[87]](#cite_note-89)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Muenchen-Allach_Dachau_sub-camp_1945-04-30_Nr_18145_ushmm.jpg/250px-Muenchen-Allach_Dachau_sub-camp_1945-04-30_Nr_18145_ushmm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Muenchen-Allach_Dachau_sub-camp_1945-04-30_Nr_18145_ushmm.jpg)

Liberated survivors of the [Munich-Allach concentration camp](/wiki/Munich-Allach_concentration_camp "Munich-Allach concentration camp") greet arriving U.S. troops, 30 April 1945

During the war, Munich was the location of multiple [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour_under_German_rule_during_World_War_II "Forced labour under German rule during World War II") camps, including two *Polenlager* camps for [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") youth,[[88]](#cite_note-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91) and 40 subcamps of the [Dachau concentration camp](/wiki/Dachau_concentration_camp "Dachau concentration camp"), in which men and women of various nationalities were held.[[90]](#cite_note-92) With up to 17,000 prisoners in 1945, the largest subcamp of Dachau was the [Munich-Allach concentration camp](/wiki/Munich-Allach_concentration_camp "Munich-Allach concentration camp").

Munich was the base of the [White Rose](/wiki/White_Rose "White Rose"), a student [resistance movement](/wiki/Resistance_movement "Resistance movement"). The group had distributed leaflets in several cities and following the 1943 [Battle of Stalingrad](/wiki/Battle_of_Stalingrad "Battle of Stalingrad") members of the group [stenciled](/wiki/Stencil "Stencil") slogans such as "Down with Hitler" and "Hitler the Mass Murderer" on public buildings in Munich. The core members were arrested and executed after [Sophie Scholl](/wiki/Sophie_Scholl "Sophie Scholl") and her brother [Hans Scholl](/wiki/Hans_Scholl "Hans Scholl") were caught distributing leaflets on [Munich University](/wiki/Munich_University "Munich University") campus calling upon the youth to rise against Hitler.[[91]](#cite_note-93)

Around 50 % of city were heavily damaged by the [bombing of Munich in World War II](/wiki/Bombing_of_Munich_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Munich in World War II"), with 71 air raids over five years. After the war, large reconstruction projects restored most of the historically relevant buildings in the old town, that had suffered damage. US troops captured Munich on 30 April 1945.[[92]](#cite_note-94)

### Postwar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Postwar")]

In the aftermath of World War II, Germany and Japan were subject to [US Military](/wiki/US_Military "US Military") occupation.[[93]](#cite_note-95) Due to Polish annexation of the [Former eastern territories of Germany](/wiki/Former_eastern_territories_of_Germany "Former eastern territories of Germany") and [expulsion of Germans from all over Eastern Europe](/wiki/Flight_and_expulsion_of_Germans_(1944%E2%80%931950) "Flight and expulsion of Germans (1944–1950)"), Munich operated over a thousand refugee camps for 151,113 people in October 1946.[[94]](#cite_note-96) After US occupation Munich was completely rebuilt following a meticulous plan, which preserved its pre-war street grid, bar a few exceptions owing to then-modern traffic concepts. In 1957, Munich's population surpassed one million. The city continued to play a highly significant role in the [German](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") economy, politics and culture, giving rise to its nickname *Heimliche Hauptstadt* ("secret capital") in the decades after World War II.[[95]](#cite_note-97) In Munich, the [Bayerischer Rundfunk](/wiki/Bayerischer_Rundfunk "Bayerischer Rundfunk") began its first television broadcast in 1954.[[96]](#cite_note-98)

The [Free State of Bavaria](/wiki/Free_State_of_Bavaria "Free State of Bavaria") used the [arms industry](/wiki/Arms_industry "Arms industry") as kernel for its [high tech](/wiki/High_tech "High tech") development policy.[[97]](#cite_note-Making_Competitive_Cities-99) Since 1963, Munich has been hosting the [Munich Security Conference](/wiki/Munich_Security_Conference "Munich Security Conference"), held annually in the [Hotel Bayerischer Hof](/wiki/Hotel_Bayerischer_Hof,_Munich "Hotel Bayerischer Hof, Munich").[[98]](#cite_note-100) Munich also became known on the political level due to the strong influence of Bavarian politician [Franz Josef Strauss](/wiki/Franz_Josef_Strauss "Franz Josef Strauss") from the 1960s to the 1980s. The [Munich Airport](/wiki/Munich_Airport "Munich Airport"), which commenced operations in 1992, was named in his honor.[[99]](#cite_note-101)

In the early 1960s [Dieter Kunzelmann](/wiki/Dieter_Kunzelmann "Dieter Kunzelmann") was expelled from the [Situationist International](/wiki/Situationist_International "Situationist International") and founded an influential group called *Subversive Aktion* in Munich. Kunzelmann was also active in West Berlin, and became known for using situationist avant-garde as a cover for political violence.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Villa_Ol%C3%ADmpica%2C_M%C3%BAnich%2C_Alemania_2012-04-28%2C_DD_01.JPG/250px-Villa_Ol%C3%ADmpica%2C_M%C3%BAnich%2C_Alemania_2012-04-28%2C_DD_01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Villa_Ol%C3%ADmpica,_M%C3%BAnich,_Alemania_2012-04-28,_DD_01.JPG)

A view from the Olympic Tower ([Olympiaturm](/wiki/Olympiaturm "Olympiaturm")) of the adjacent [Olympic Village](/wiki/Olympic_Village,_Munich "Olympic Village, Munich")

Munich hosted the [1972 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics"). After winning the bid in 1966 the [Mayor of Munich](/wiki/Mayor_of_Munich "Mayor of Munich") [Hans-Jochen Vogel](/wiki/Hans-Jochen_Vogel "Hans-Jochen Vogel") accelerated the construction of the [U-Bahn](/wiki/Munich_U-Bahn "Munich U-Bahn") subway and the [S-Bahn](/wiki/Munich_S-Bahn "Munich S-Bahn") metropolitan commuter railway. In May 1967 the construction work began for a new U-Bahn line connecting the city with the [Olympic Park](/wiki/Olympiapark_(Munich) "Olympiapark (Munich)"). The Olympic Park subway station was built near the [BMW Headquarters](/wiki/BMW_Headquarters "BMW Headquarters") and the line was completed in May 1972, three months before the opening of the 1972 Summer Olympics. Shortly before the opening ceremony, Munich also inaugurated a sizable pedestrian priority zone between [Karlsplatz](/wiki/Karlsplatz_(Stachus) "Karlsplatz (Stachus)") and [Marienplatz](/wiki/Marienplatz "Marienplatz").[[101]](#cite_note-103) In 1970 the Munich city council released funds so that the iconic [gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") facade and [Glockenspiel](/wiki/Rathaus-Glockenspiel "Rathaus-Glockenspiel") of the [*New City Hall*](/wiki/Neues_Rathaus_(Munich) "Neues Rathaus (Munich)") (Neues Rathaus) could be restored.[[102]](#cite_note-104)

During the 1972 Summer Olympics 11 Israeli athletes were murdered by [Palestinian](/wiki/Palestinian_people "Palestinian people") terrorists in the [Munich massacre](/wiki/Munich_massacre "Munich massacre"), when gunmen from the Palestinian "[Black September](/wiki/Black_September_(group) "Black September (group)")" group took hostage members of the Israeli Olympic team.[[103]](#cite_note-105)

The most deadly militant attack the [Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Germany "Federal Republic of Germany") has ever witnessed was the [Oktoberfest bombing](/wiki/Oktoberfest_bombing "Oktoberfest bombing"). The attack was eventually blamed on militant [Neo-Nazism](/wiki/Neo-Nazism "Neo-Nazism").[[104]](#cite_note-106)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Nockherberg_M%C3%BCnchen_Paulaner_Biergarten.jpg/250px-Nockherberg_M%C3%BCnchen_Paulaner_Biergarten.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nockherberg_M%C3%BCnchen_Paulaner_Biergarten.jpg)

The [Nockherberg](/wiki/Nockherberg "Nockherberg") beer garden

Munich and its [urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl") emerged as the leading German high tech region during the 1980s and 1990s. The urban economy of Munich became characterized by a dynamic [labour market](/wiki/Labour_market "Labour market"), low unemployment, a growing [service economy](/wiki/Service_economy "Service economy") and high per capita income.[[97]](#cite_note-Making_Competitive_Cities-99) Munich is home of the famous [Nockherberg](/wiki/Nockherberg "Nockherberg") Strong Beer Festival during the Lenten fasting period (usually in March). Its origins go back to the 17th/18th century, but has become popular when the festivities were first televised in the 1980s. The fest includes comical speeches and a mini-musical in which numerous German politicians are parodied by look-alike actors.[[105]](#cite_note-107)

In 2007 the [ecological restoration](/wiki/Ecological_restoration "Ecological restoration") of the river Isar in the urban area of Munich was awarded the Water Development Prize by the German Association for Water, Wastewater and Waste (known as DWA in German). The renaturation of the Isar allows for the near natural development of the [river bed](/wiki/River_bed "River bed") and is part of Munich's [flood protection](/wiki/Flood_protection "Flood protection").[[106]](#cite_note-108) About 20% of buildings in Munich now have a [green roof](/wiki/Green_roof "Green roof"). Munich city council has been encouraging better [stormwater](/wiki/Stormwater "Stormwater") management since the 1990s with regulations and subsidies.[[107]](#cite_note-109)

On the fifth anniversary of the [2011 Norway attacks](/wiki/2011_Norway_attacks "2011 Norway attacks") an [active shooter](/wiki/Active_shooter "Active shooter") perpetrated a [hate crime](/wiki/Hate_crime "Hate crime"). The [2016 Munich shooting](/wiki/2016_Munich_shooting "2016 Munich shooting") targeted people of Turkish and Arab descent.[[108]](#cite_note-110)

Munich was one of the host cities for [UEFA Euro 2020](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2020 "UEFA Euro 2020"), which was delayed for a year due to the [COVID-19 pandemic in Germany](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Germany "COVID-19 pandemic in Germany"), and was a host city for [UEFA Euro 2024](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2024 "UEFA Euro 2024").[[109]](#cite_note-111)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Munich_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-06-12.jpg/250px-Munich_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-06-12.jpg)](/wiki/File:Munich_by_Sentinel-2,_2020-06-12.jpg)

Satellite photo by ESA Sentinel-2

### Topography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Topography")]

Munich lies on the elevated plains of [Upper Bavaria](/wiki/Upper_Bavaria "Upper Bavaria"), about 50 km (31 mi) north of the northern edge of the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"), at an altitude of about 520 m (1,706 ft) [ASL](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level"). The local rivers are the [Isar](/wiki/Isar "Isar") and the [Würm](/wiki/W%C3%BCrm_(Amper) "Würm (Amper)"). Munich is situated in the Northern [Alpine Foreland](/wiki/Foothills "Foothills"). The northern part of this sandy plateau includes a highly fertile [flint](/wiki/Flint "Flint") area which is no longer affected by the [folding](/wiki/Fold_(geology) "Fold (geology)") processes found in the Alps, while the southern part is covered with [morainic](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine") hills. Between these are fields of [fluvio-glacial](/wiki/Fluvio-glacial "Fluvio-glacial") out-wash, such as around Munich. Wherever these deposits get thinner, the [ground water](/wiki/Groundwater "Groundwater") can permeate the gravel surface and flood the area, leading to [marshes](/wiki/Marsh "Marsh") as in the north of Munich.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Climate")]

According to the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), the climate is [oceanic](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") (*Cfb*) bordering on [humid continental](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (*Dfb*), with features like warm to hot summers and cold winters, but without permanent snow cover.[[110]](#cite_note-112)[[111]](#cite_note-113) The city centre lies between both climates, while the [airport of Munich](/wiki/Munich_Airport "Munich Airport") has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate"). The warmest month, on average, is July. The coolest is January.

The proximity to the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") brings higher volumes of rainfall and consequently greater susceptibility to [flood problems](/wiki/Urban_flooding "Urban flooding"). Studies of [adaptation to climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_adaptation "Climate change adaptation") and extreme events are carried out; one of them is the Isar Plan of the [EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") Adaptation Climate.[[112]](#cite_note-114)

Showers and thunderstorms bring the highest average monthly precipitation in late spring and throughout the summer. The most precipitation occurs in July, on average. Winter tends to have less precipitation, the least in February.

The higher elevation and proximity to the Alps cause the city to have more rain and snow than many other parts of Germany. The Alps affect the city's climate in other ways too; for example, the warm downhill wind from the Alps ([föhn wind](/wiki/F%C3%B6hn_wind "Föhn wind")), which can raise temperatures sharply within a few hours even in the winter.

Being at the centre of Europe, Munich is subject to many climatic influences, so that weather conditions there are more variable than in other European cities, especially those further west and south of the Alps.

Munich is near the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps"). Annual variation in temperature can be significant, because there are no large bodies of water nearby. The winter in Munich is generally cold and overcast, and some Munich winters have significant snow. January is the coldest month. While winter averages remain only moderately cold, and relatively mild for an elevated inland location of Munich's latitude, [inversion](/wiki/Inversion_(meteorology) "Inversion (meteorology)") from the nearby Alps causes cold air to sink and result in temperatures below −15 °C (5 °F). In Munich the summer is usually pleasantly warm, with daytime temperatures averaging 25 °C (77 °F).

Munich is subject to active [convective](/wiki/Convective "Convective") seasons and sometimes damaging events. The Alpine [thunderstorm system](/wiki/Thunderstorm "Thunderstorm") moves along the mountain range, or detaches, heading east-north-east over the foothills of the Alps.[[113]](#cite_note-115)

At Munich's official [weather stations](/wiki/Weather_station "Weather station"), the highest and lowest temperatures ever measured are 37.5 °C (100 °F), on 27 July 1983 in Trudering-Riem, and −31.6 °C (−24.9 °F), on 12 February 1929 in the Botanic Garden of the city.[[114]](#cite_note-116)[[115]](#cite_note-117)

#### Climate change

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Climate change")]

In Munich, the general trend of [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming") with a rise of medium yearly temperatures of about 1 °C (1.8 °F) in Germany between 1900 and 2020 can be observed as well. In November 2016 the city council concluded officially that a further rise in medium temperature, a higher number of heat extremes, a rise in the number of hot days and nights with temperatures higher than 20 °C ([tropical nights](/wiki/Tropical_night "Tropical night")), a change in [precipitation patterns](/wiki/Precipitation "Precipitation"), as well as a rise in the number of local instances of heavy rain, is to be expected as part of the ongoing climate change. The city administration decided to support a joint study from its own Referat für Gesundheit und Umwelt (department for health and environmental issues) and the [German Meteorological Service](/wiki/Deutscher_Wetterdienst "Deutscher Wetterdienst") that will gather data on local weather. The data is supposed to be used to create a plan for action for adapting the city to better deal with climate change as well as an integrated action program for climate protection in Munich. With the help of those programs issues regarding [spatial planning](/wiki/Spatial_planning "Spatial planning") and settlement density, the development of buildings and green spaces as well as plans for functioning [ventilation](/wiki/Ventilation_(architecture) "Ventilation (architecture)") in a cityscape can be monitored and managed.[[120]](#cite_note-122)

From only 24,000 inhabitants in 1700, the city population doubled about every 30 years. It was 100,000 in 1852, 250,000 in 1883 and 500,000 in 1901. Since then, Munich has become Germany's third-largest city. In 1933, 840,901 inhabitants were counted, and in 1957 over 1 million. Munich has reached 1.5 million in 2022.

### Immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Immigration")]

In December 2023, Munich had 1.58 million inhabitants; 477,855 foreign nationals resided in the city as of 31 December 2023 with 42.88% of these residents being citizens of EU member states, and 29.66% citizens in European states not in the EU (including Kosovo and Turkey).[[121]](#cite_note-123) Along with the Turks, the Croats are one of the two largest foreign minorities in the city, which is why some Croats refer to Munich as their "second capital".[[122]](#cite_note-124) The largest groups of foreign nationals were [Turks](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people") (38,947), [Croats](/wiki/Croats "Croats") (37,541), [Italians](/wiki/Italians "Italians") (28,142), [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") (24,843), [Bosnians](/wiki/Bosnians "Bosnians") (24,161), [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") (21,899), and [Austrians](/wiki/Austrians "Austrians") (21,944).

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Religion")]

About 45% of Munich's residents are not affiliated with any religious group; this ratio represents the fastest growing segment of the population. As in the rest of Germany, the Catholic and Protestant churches have experienced a continuous decline in membership. As of 31 December 2017, 31.8% of the city's inhabitants were [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic"), 11.4% [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"), 0.3% Jewish (see: [History of the Jews in Munich](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Munich "History of the Jews in Munich")),[[124]](#cite_note-126) and 3.6% were members of an Orthodox Church ([Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") or [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy")).[[125]](#cite_note-127) About 1% adhere to other Christian denominations. There is also a small [Old Catholic](/wiki/Old_Catholic "Old Catholic") parish and an English-speaking parish of the [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)") in the city. According to Munich Statistical Office, in 2013 about 6.9% of Munich's population was [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim").[[126]](#cite_note-128)
Munich has the largest [Uyghur](/wiki/Uyghurs "Uyghurs") population with about 800 (whole Germany about 1,600) people with Uyghur diaspora. Many of them fled to Munich due to the Chinese government and are exiled in Munich. Munich is also home to [World Uyghur Congress](/wiki/World_Uyghur_Congress "World Uyghur Congress"), which is an [international organisation](/wiki/International_organisation "International organisation") of exiled Uyghurs.[[127]](#cite_note-129)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Bayerische_Staatskanzlei_Munich_2014_02.jpg/250px-Bayerische_Staatskanzlei_Munich_2014_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bayerische_Staatskanzlei_Munich_2014_02.jpg)

[Bavarian State Chancellery](/wiki/Bayerische_Staatskanzlei "Bayerische Staatskanzlei")

As the capital of Bavaria, Munich is an important political centre for both the state and country as a whole. It is the seat of the [Landtag of Bavaria](/wiki/Landtag_of_Bavaria "Landtag of Bavaria"), the [State Chancellery](/wiki/Bayerische_Staatskanzlei "Bayerische Staatskanzlei"), and all state departments. Several national and international authorities are located in Munich, including the [Federal Finance Court of Germany](/wiki/Federal_Finance_Court_of_Germany "Federal Finance Court of Germany"), the [German Patent Office](/wiki/German_Patent_Office "German Patent Office") and the [European Patent Office](/wiki/European_Patent_Office "European Patent Office"). Bavaria has been dominated by the [Christian Social Union in Bavaria](/wiki/Christian_Social_Union_in_Bavaria "Christian Social Union in Bavaria") (CSU) on a federal, state, and local level since the establishment of the Federal Republic in 1949.

### Mayor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Mayor")]

The current mayor of Munich is [Dieter Reiter](/wiki/Dieter_Reiter "Dieter Reiter"), he is from the [Social Democratic Party of Germany](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") (SPD). He was elected in 2014 and re-elected in 2020. He will be succeeded by [Dominik Krause](/wiki/Dominik_Krause "Dominik Krause") of the [the Greens](/wiki/Alliance_90/The_Greens "Alliance 90/The Greens") on 1 May 2026.

The most recent mayoral election was held on 8 March 2026, with a runoff held on 22 March, and the results were as follows:

The governing coalition after the 2020 election consisted of the Greens and the SPD, as well as the allied Volt and Pink List, which had one seat each. While the Greens had gained the most seats in the city council, the mayor was from the SPD. The parties made an agreement including social and ecological goals and focusing on heightening stipulations for new development in the city. The Red-Green alliance is a reprise of the 1990–2014 coalition, which was interrupted by a six-year CSU-SPD majority from 2014 to 2020.[[129]](#cite_note-p829-132)[[130]](#cite_note-x533-133)

### Subdivisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Subdivisions")]

Main article: [Boroughs of Munich](/wiki/Boroughs_of_Munich "Boroughs of Munich")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Stadtbezirke_Lage_in_M%C3%BCnchen.png/250px-Stadtbezirke_Lage_in_M%C3%BCnchen.png)](/wiki/File:Stadtbezirke_Lage_in_M%C3%BCnchen.png)

Munich's boroughs

Since the reform of 1992, Munich is divided into 25 administrative [boroughs](/wiki/Borough "Borough") (*Stadtbezirke*). They are subdivided into 105 statistical areas.

[Allach-Untermenzing](/wiki/Allach-Untermenzing "Allach-Untermenzing") (23), [Altstadt-Lehel](/wiki/Altstadt-Lehel "Altstadt-Lehel") (1), [Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied](/wiki/Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied "Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied") (22), [Au-Haidhausen](/wiki/Au-Haidhausen "Au-Haidhausen") (5), [Berg am Laim](/wiki/Berg_am_Laim "Berg am Laim") (14), [Bogenhausen](/wiki/Bogenhausen "Bogenhausen") (13), [Feldmoching-Hasenbergl](/wiki/Feldmoching-Hasenbergl "Feldmoching-Hasenbergl") (24), [Hadern](/wiki/Hadern "Hadern") (20), [Laim](/wiki/Laim "Laim") (25), [Ludwigsvorstadt-Isarvorstadt](/wiki/Ludwigsvorstadt-Isarvorstadt "Ludwigsvorstadt-Isarvorstadt") (2), [Maxvorstadt](/wiki/Maxvorstadt "Maxvorstadt") (3), [Milbertshofen-Am Hart](/wiki/Milbertshofen-Am_Hart "Milbertshofen-Am Hart") (11), [Moosach](/wiki/Moosach_(District_of_Munich) "Moosach (District of Munich)") (10), [Neuhausen-Nymphenburg](/wiki/Neuhausen-Nymphenburg "Neuhausen-Nymphenburg") (9), [Obergiesing](/wiki/Obergiesing "Obergiesing") (17), [Pasing-Obermenzing](/wiki/Pasing-Obermenzing "Pasing-Obermenzing") (21), [Ramersdorf-Perlach](/wiki/Ramersdorf-Perlach "Ramersdorf-Perlach") (16), [Schwabing-Freimann](/wiki/Schwabing-Freimann "Schwabing-Freimann") (12), [Schwabing-West](/wiki/Schwabing-West "Schwabing-West") (4), [Schwanthalerhöhe](/wiki/Schwanthalerh%C3%B6he "Schwanthalerhöhe") (8), [Sendling](/wiki/Sendling "Sendling") (6), [Sendling-Westpark](/wiki/Sendling-Westpark "Sendling-Westpark") (7), [Thalkirchen-Obersendling-Forstenried-Fürstenried-Solln](/wiki/Thalkirchen-Obersendling-Forstenried-F%C3%BCrstenried-Solln "Thalkirchen-Obersendling-Forstenried-Fürstenried-Solln") (19), [Trudering-Riem](/wiki/Trudering-Riem "Trudering-Riem") (15), and [Untergiesing-Harlaching](/wiki/Untergiesing-Harlaching "Untergiesing-Harlaching") (18).

There is no official division into districts. The number of districts is about 50, and if smaller units are counted as well, there are about 90 to 100 (see [map](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Liste_der_Stadtteile_M%C3%BCnchens#/media/Datei:Karte_der_Ortsbezeichnungen_in_München.png "de:Liste der Stadtteile Münchens")). The three largest districts are [Schwabing](/wiki/Schwabing "Schwabing") in the north (about 110,000 inhabitants), [Sendling](/wiki/Sendling "Sendling") in the southwest (about 100,000 inhabitants), and [Giesing](/wiki/Giesing "Giesing") in the south (about 80,000 inhabitants).[[131]](#cite_note-134)
