### Old Town

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Old Town")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Ruffiniblock_PettenbeckstrNordseite_2020_n._Renovierung.jpg/250px-Ruffiniblock_PettenbeckstrNordseite_2020_n._Renovierung.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ruffiniblock_PettenbeckstrNordseite_2020_n._Renovierung.jpg)

The [Ruffinihaus](/wiki/Ruffinihaus "Ruffinihaus") at [Rindermarkt](/wiki/Rindermarkt "Rindermarkt")

At the centre of the old town is the [Marienplatz](/wiki/Marienplatz "Marienplatz") with the [Old Town Hall](/wiki/Old_Town_Hall,_Munich "Old Town Hall, Munich") and the [New Town Hall](/wiki/New_Town_Hall,_Munich "New Town Hall, Munich"). Its tower contains the [Rathaus-Glockenspiel](/wiki/Rathaus-Glockenspiel "Rathaus-Glockenspiel"). The [Peterskirche](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Church,_Munich "St. Peter's Church, Munich") is the oldest church of the inner city. Nearby St. Peter, the Gothic hall-church [Heiliggeistkirche](/wiki/Heiliggeistkirche_(Munich) "Heiliggeistkirche (Munich)") was converted to baroque style from 1724 onwards and looks down upon the [Viktualienmarkt](/wiki/Viktualienmarkt "Viktualienmarkt"). Three gates of the demolished medieval fortification survive; these are the [Isartor](/wiki/Isartor "Isartor"), the [Sendlinger Tor](/wiki/Sendlinger_Tor "Sendlinger Tor"), and the [Karlstor](/wiki/Karlstor "Karlstor"). The Karlstor leads up to the [Stachus](/wiki/Karlsplatz_(Stachus) "Karlsplatz (Stachus)"), a square dominated by the [Justizpalast](/wiki/Justizpalast_(Munich) "Justizpalast (Munich)") (Palace of Justice).

The [Frauenkirche](/wiki/Munich_Frauenkirche "Munich Frauenkirche") serves as the cathedral for the [Catholic Archdiocese of Munich and Freising](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Munich_and_Freising "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Munich and Freising"). The nearby [Michaelskirche](/wiki/St._Michael%27s_Church,_Munich "St. Michael's Church, Munich") is the largest [renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") church north of the Alps, while the [Theatinerkirche](/wiki/Theatine_Church,_Munich "Theatine Church, Munich") is a [basilica](/wiki/Basilica "Basilica") in Italianate high baroque, which had a major influence on southern German [baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") architecture. Its dome dominates the [Odeonsplatz](/wiki/Odeonsplatz "Odeonsplatz").

### Palaces and castles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Palaces and castles")]

Schloss Nymphenburg ([Nymphenburg Palace](/wiki/Nymphenburg_Palace "Nymphenburg Palace"), construction started 1664) is a museum open to the public for tours.[[132]](#cite_note-Zen_2020_i476-135)[[133]](#cite_note-Lonely_Planet_2010_b951-136)

The smaller Schloss Fürstenried ([Fürstenried Palace](/wiki/F%C3%BCrstenried_Palace "Fürstenried Palace"), construction 1715–1717) is used by the [Archdiocese of Munich and Freising](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Munich_and_Freising "Archdiocese of Munich and Freising") as a conference location.[[134]](#cite_note-muenchen.de_2022_v258-137)

Schloss Blutenburg ([Blutenburg Castle](/wiki/Blutenburg_Castle "Blutenburg Castle")) opened as a children's library in 2024,[[135]](#cite_note-Hordych_2024_l233-138) but visitors may tour the late-Gothic Blutenburg Castle Church built on the same grounds.[[136]](#cite_note-muenchen.de_2022_i705-139)

The large [Munich Residenz](/wiki/Munich_Residenz "Munich Residenz") complex on the edge of Munich's Old Town now ranks among Europe's most significant museums of interior decoration. Within the Residenz is the splendid [Cuvilliés Theatre](/wiki/Cuvilli%C3%A9s_Theatre "Cuvilliés Theatre") and next door is the [National Theatre Munich](/wiki/National_Theatre_Munich "National Theatre Munich"). Among the mansions that still exist in Munich are the [Palais Porcia](/wiki/Palais_Porcia "Palais Porcia"), the [Palais Preysing](/wiki/Palais_Preysing "Palais Preysing"), the [Palais Holnstein](/wiki/Palais_Holnstein "Palais Holnstein") and the [Prinz-Carl-Palais](/wiki/Prinz-Carl-Palais "Prinz-Carl-Palais"). All mansions are situated close to the Residenz, so is the [Alter Hof](/wiki/Alter_Hof "Alter Hof"), the first residence of the [House of Wittelsbach](/wiki/House_of_Wittelsbach "House of Wittelsbach").

### Modernist architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Modernist architecture")]

Despite Munich being the breeding ground for German [Jugendstil](/wiki/Jugendstil "Jugendstil"), starting with the architect [Martin Dülfer](/wiki/Martin_D%C3%BClfer "Martin Dülfer"), Munich Jugendstil style was quickly submerged as historic trash. While the modernist architect [Theodor Fischer](/wiki/Theodor_Fischer "Theodor Fischer") was based in Munich, his influence on Munich underwhelmed. Prior to 1914 the city of Munich was under-industrialized. During the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic"), the Munich establishment was hostile to [modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism"). The TUM professor [German Bestelmeyer](/wiki/German_Bestelmeyer "German Bestelmeyer") favored a conservative style, and [Jacobus Oud](/wiki/Jacobus_Oud "Jacobus Oud") was rejected for the post of city building chief. Modernist exceptions include a series of post offices by [Robert Vorhoelzer](/wiki/Robert_Vorhoelzer "Robert Vorhoelzer") built in the late 1920s and early 1930s. Examples of [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") temporary constructions include the *Wohnmaschine* (*Housing Machine*) by [Robert Vorhoelzer](/wiki/Robert_Vorhoelzer "Robert Vorhoelzer"), as well as the *Flachdachhaus* (*Flat Roof House*) by [Fritz Norkauer](/w/index.php?title=Fritz_Norkauer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fritz Norkauer (page does not exist)"). [Paul Schultze-Naumburg](/wiki/Paul_Schultze-Naumburg "Paul Schultze-Naumburg"), and the [Kampfbund](/wiki/Kampfbund "Kampfbund") enjoyed particular popularity.[[137]](#cite_note-140)

### High rise buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: High rise buildings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/GraphyArchy_-_Wiki00002.jpg/250px-GraphyArchy_-_Wiki00002.jpg)](/wiki/File:GraphyArchy_-_Wiki00002.jpg)

The [HVB Tower](/wiki/Hypo-Haus "Hypo-Haus") at Arabellapark

Several high-rise buildings are clustered at the northern edge of Munich in the skyline, like the [HVB Tower](/wiki/Hypo-Haus "Hypo-Haus"), the [Arabella High-Rise Building](/wiki/Arabella_High-Rise_Building "Arabella High-Rise Building"), the [Highlight Towers](/wiki/Highlight_Towers "Highlight Towers"), [Uptown Munich](/wiki/Uptown_Munich "Uptown Munich"), Münchner Tor and the [BMW Headquarters](/wiki/BMW_Headquarters "BMW Headquarters") next to the [Olympic Park](/wiki/Olympiapark_(Munich) "Olympiapark (Munich)"). Further high-rise buildings are located in the [Werksviertel](/w/index.php?title=Werksviertel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Werksviertel (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Werksviertel "de:Werksviertel")] in [Berg am Laim](/wiki/Berg_am_Laim "Berg am Laim").

### Long-term residential development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Long-term residential development")]

Munich is subject to a long-term residential development plan that is established by the city administration of Munich. The LaSie ("Langfristige Siedlungsentwicklung") was passed in 2011 in response to the acute housing crisis. LaSie is aligned with the strategic development plan passed for Munich in 1998 ("Perspektive München"). LaSie defines three priorities for the construction of residential housing in Munich. Existing [housing estates](/wiki/Housing_estate "Housing estate"), post-war low-density developments, and the suburban area are subject to [densification](/wiki/Densification "Densification") ("Nachverdichtung"). Non-residential industrial areas are subject to conservation and will be turned into residential and mixed-use areas. On greenfield sites in the Munich periphery medium and large-scale housing estates are to be built so as to extend Munich's urban center.[[138]](#cite_note-141)

## Parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Parks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Munich_Olympiapark_Public_Viewing_SCG-NED_%280-1%29.jpg/250px-Munich_Olympiapark_Public_Viewing_SCG-NED_%280-1%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Munich_Olympiapark_Public_Viewing_SCG-NED_(0-1).jpg)

Olympiapark, public viewing during [FIFA World Cup 2006](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup_2006 "FIFA World Cup 2006")

[Friedrich Ludwig von Sckell](/wiki/Friedrich_Ludwig_von_Sckell "Friedrich Ludwig von Sckell") became famous for designing the [Englischer Garten](/wiki/Englischer_Garten "Englischer Garten") between 1789 and 1807. Besides planning the first public garden in Europe, Sckell also redesigned Baroque gardens as landscape gardens, including the parks of [Nymphenburg Palace](/wiki/Nymphenburg_Palace "Nymphenburg Palace") and the [Botanischer Garten München-Nymphenburg](/wiki/Botanischer_Garten_M%C3%BCnchen-Nymphenburg "Botanischer Garten München-Nymphenburg").[[139]](#cite_note-142)

Other large green spaces are the [Olympiapark](/wiki/Olympiapark,_Munich "Olympiapark, Munich"), the [Westpark](/wiki/Westpark_(Munich) "Westpark (Munich)") and the [Ostpark](/wiki/Ostpark_(Munich) "Ostpark (Munich)"). The city's oldest park is the [Hofgarten](/wiki/Hofgarten_(Munich) "Hofgarten (Munich)"), near the Residenz, dating back to the 16th century. The site of the largest beer garden in town, the former royal Hirschgarten, was founded in 1780.[[140]](#cite_note-143)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main article: [Sport in Munich](/wiki/Sport_in_Munich "Sport in Munich")

### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Football")]

Main article: [Football in Munich](/wiki/Football_in_Munich "Football in Munich")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Allianz_Arena_2008-02-09.jpg/250px-Allianz_Arena_2008-02-09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Allianz_Arena_2008-02-09.jpg)

[Allianz Arena](/wiki/Allianz_Arena "Allianz Arena"), also the home stadium of [FC Bayern Munich](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich "FC Bayern Munich")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/M%C3%BCnchen_-_Olympische_Bauten.jpg/250px-M%C3%BCnchen_-_Olympische_Bauten.jpg)](/wiki/File:M%C3%BCnchen_-_Olympische_Bauten.jpg)

Olympiasee in Olympiapark, Munich

Munich is home to several professional Association football teams including the [FC Bayern Munich](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich "FC Bayern Munich"). Other notable clubs include [1860 Munich](/wiki/TSV_1860_Munich "TSV 1860 Munich"), who currently play in the [3. Liga](/wiki/3._Liga "3. Liga"). Noticeably, [FC Bayern Munich](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich "FC Bayern Munich") is the most successful club in Germany and it is also very reputed across Europe and the world. Munich hosted matches in the [2006 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup").[[141]](#cite_note-144)

### Basketball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Basketball")]

[FC Bayern Munich Basketball](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich_(basketball) "FC Bayern Munich (basketball)") is currently playing in the Beko Basket Bundesliga. The city hosted the final stages of the FIBA [EuroBasket 1993](/wiki/EuroBasket_1993 "EuroBasket 1993"), where the [German national basketball team](/wiki/German_national_basketball_team "German national basketball team") won the gold medal.

### Ice hockey

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Ice hockey")]

The city's ice hockey club is [EHC Red Bull München](/wiki/EHC_Red_Bull_M%C3%BCnchen "EHC Red Bull München") who play in the [Deutsche Eishockey Liga](/wiki/Deutsche_Eishockey_Liga "Deutsche Eishockey Liga"). The team has won four DEL Championships, in 2016, 2017, 2018 and 2023.

### Olympics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Olympics")]

Munich hosted the [1972 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics"); the [Munich massacre](/wiki/Munich_massacre "Munich massacre") took place in the [Olympic village](/wiki/Olympic_Village,_Munich "Olympic Village, Munich"). It was one of the host cities for the [2006 Football World Cup](/wiki/2006_FIFA_World_Cup "2006 FIFA World Cup"), which was not held in Munich's [Olympic Stadium](/wiki/Olympic_Stadium_(Munich) "Olympic Stadium (Munich)"), but in a new [football specific stadium](/wiki/Soccer-specific_stadium "Soccer-specific stadium"), the [Allianz Arena](/wiki/Allianz_Arena "Allianz Arena"). Munich bid to host the [2018 Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/2018_Winter_Olympic_Games "2018 Winter Olympic Games"), but lost to [Pyeongchang](/wiki/Pyeongchang_County "Pyeongchang County").[[142]](#cite_note-145)

In October 2025, Munich citizens voted heavily (66%)[[143]](#cite_note-146) in favor of their city's bid to host the 2036, 2040, or 2044 Olympic Games. The pro-bid campaign[[144]](#cite_note-147) focused on ecological and sustainable solutions, social housing, and leveraging existing infrastructure.

### Road running

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Road running")]

Regular annual road running events in Munich are the [Munich Marathon](/wiki/Munich_Marathon "Munich Marathon") in October, the Stadtlauf end of June, the company run B2Run in July, the New Year's Run on 31 December, the [Spartan Race](/wiki/Spartan_Race "Spartan Race") Sprint, the Olympia Alm Crosslauf and the Bestzeitenmarathon.

### Swimming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Swimming")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/bb/Muc_OlympiaSchwimmhalle_2013.jpg/250px-Muc_OlympiaSchwimmhalle_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Muc_OlympiaSchwimmhalle_2013.jpg)

Olympia Schwimmhalle

Public sporting facilities in Munich include ten indoor swimming pools[[145]](#cite_note-publicindoorpools-148) and eight outdoor swimming pools,[[146]](#cite_note-publicoutdoorpools-149) which are operated by the [Munich City Utilities (SWM)](/wiki/Stadtwerke_M%C3%BCnchen "Stadtwerke München") communal company.[[147]](#cite_note-SWM-150) Popular indoor swimming pools include the [Olympia Schwimmhalle](/wiki/Olympia_Schwimmhalle "Olympia Schwimmhalle") of the [1972 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics"), the wave pool Cosimawellenbad, as well as the Müllersches Volksbad which was built in 1901. Further, swimming within Munich's city limits is also possible in several artificial lakes such as for example the [Riemer See](/wiki/Riemer_See "Riemer See") or the [Langwieder lake district](/wiki/Langwieder_lake_district "Langwieder lake district").[[148]](#cite_note-lakesinmunich-151)

### River surfing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: River surfing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Eisbach_die_Welle_Surfer.JPG/250px-Eisbach_die_Welle_Surfer.JPG)](/wiki/File:Eisbach_die_Welle_Surfer.JPG)

Surfer on the Eisbach river wave

[River surfing](/wiki/River_surfing "River surfing") is a popular sport in Munich. The Flosskanal wave in the south of Munich is less challenging. A well visited surfing spot for experienced surfers is the [Eisbach](/wiki/Eisbach_(Isar) "Eisbach (Isar)") standing wave, where the annual Munich Surf Open is celebrated on the last Saturday of July.[[149]](#cite_note-152)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Language")]

Main article: [Bavarian language](/wiki/Bavarian_language "Bavarian language")

German is spoken and understood in and around Munich. While the German language has many dialects, so-called "[Standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German")" or "High German" is learned in schools and spoken among [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans"), [Austrians](/wiki/Austrians "Austrians") and in some parts of Switzerland. A speaker of a [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German") dialect in Hamburg may find it difficult to understand the dialect of a Bavarian mountaineer.[[150]](#cite_note-153) The [Bavarian dialects](/wiki/Bavarian_language "Bavarian language") are recognized as regional languages and continue to be spoken alongside Standard German.[[151]](#cite_note-154)

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Museums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Deutsches_Museum_Portrait_4.jpg/250px-Deutsches_Museum_Portrait_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deutsches_Museum_Portrait_4.jpg)

Deutsches Museum

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Glyptothek_in_M%C3%BCnchen_in_2013.jpg/250px-Glyptothek_in_M%C3%BCnchen_in_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glyptothek_in_M%C3%BCnchen_in_2013.jpg)

The [Glyptothek](/wiki/Glyptothek "Glyptothek")

The gothic [Morris dancers](/wiki/Morris_dance "Morris dance") of [Erasmus Grasser](/wiki/Erasmus_Grasser "Erasmus Grasser") are exhibited in the [Munich City Museum](/wiki/Munich_Stadtmuseum "Munich Stadtmuseum") in the old gothic arsenal building in the inner city.

In 1903 [Oskar von Miller](/wiki/Oskar_von_Miller "Oskar von Miller") assembled a group of engineers and industrialists, who chartered the [Deutsches Museum](/wiki/Deutsches_Museum "Deutsches Museum"). The Museum was built with the financial support of the German business and imperial nobility community, as well as the blessing of [Wilhelm II, German Emperor](/wiki/Wilhelm_II,_German_Emperor "Wilhelm II, German Emperor").[[152]](#cite_note-155) The Deutsches Museum had its grand opening in 1925, but has undergone a reinvention recently. The Deutsches Museum now operates three locations. The original site in central Munich continues to expand its exhibits.[[153]](#cite_note-156)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Bayerisches_Nationalmuseum_-_Muenchen_-_2013.jpg/250px-Bayerisches_Nationalmuseum_-_Muenchen_-_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bayerisches_Nationalmuseum_-_Muenchen_-_2013.jpg)

[Bavarian National Museum](/wiki/Bavarian_National_Museum "Bavarian National Museum")

The city has several important art galleries, most of which can be found in the [Kunstareal](/wiki/Kunstareal "Kunstareal"). The [Lenbachhaus](/wiki/Lenbachhaus "Lenbachhaus") displays works of the movement [Der Blaue Reiter](/wiki/Der_Blaue_Reiter "Der Blaue Reiter") (The Blue Rider), a Munich-based modernist art.[[154]](#cite_note-157) Starting in the 1970s, German municipalities started to respond to cultural tourism and invested in public museums. The [Neue Pinakothek](/wiki/Neue_Pinakothek "Neue Pinakothek"), like other German museums, was wholly reconstructed from 1974 until 1981.[[155]](#cite_note-158) The [Pinakothek der Moderne](/wiki/Pinakothek_der_Moderne "Pinakothek der Moderne") exhibits an eclectic mix of [contemporary art](/wiki/Contemporary_art "Contemporary art"). The principal focus of its permanent collection is Classical Moderns, but the displays are enhanced continuously with gifts from private collections.[[156]](#cite_note-159)

City guides published in the early 1860s directed tourists to Munich's architecture and art collections, which at the time were unique in Germany and are a legacy mainly of [Ludwig I of Bavaria](/wiki/Ludwig_I_of_Bavaria "Ludwig I of Bavaria"), with contributions from [Maximilian II of Bavaria](/wiki/Maximilian_II_of_Bavaria "Maximilian II of Bavaria").[[157]](#cite_note-160) The Alte Pinakothek contains works of European masters between the 14th and 18th centuries. Major displays include [Albrecht Dürer](/wiki/Albrecht_D%C3%BCrer "Albrecht Dürer")'s [*Self-Portrait* (1500)](/wiki/Self-Portrait_(D%C3%BCrer,_Munich) "Self-Portrait (Dürer, Munich)"), his *[Four Apostles](/wiki/The_Four_Apostles "The Four Apostles")*, [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael")'s paintings *[The Canigiani Holy Family](/wiki/Canigiani_Holy_Family_(Raphael) "Canigiani Holy Family (Raphael)")* and *[Madonna Tempi](/wiki/Tempi_Madonna_(Raphael) "Tempi Madonna (Raphael)")* as well as [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens") large *Judgment Day*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/BMW_Welt%2C_M%C3%BAnich%2C_Alemania16.jpg/250px-BMW_Welt%2C_M%C3%BAnich%2C_Alemania16.jpg)](/wiki/File:BMW_Welt,_M%C3%BAnich,_Alemania16.jpg)

[BMW Welt](/wiki/BMW_Welt "BMW Welt")

An extensive collection of Greek and Roman art is held in the [Glyptothek](/wiki/Glyptothek "Glyptothek")[[158]](#cite_note-161) and the [Staatliche Antikensammlungen](/wiki/Staatliche_Antikensammlungen "Staatliche Antikensammlungen") (the State Antiquities Collections). Works on display include the [Medusa Rondanini](/wiki/Medusa_Rondanini "Medusa Rondanini"), the [Barberini Faun](/wiki/Barberini_Faun "Barberini Faun") and figures from the [Temple of Aphaea](/wiki/Temple_of_Aphaea "Temple of Aphaea") on [Aegina](/wiki/Aegina "Aegina") for the Glyptothek.[[159]](#cite_note-162) Another interesting museum is the [Staatliche Sammlung für Ägyptische Kunst](/wiki/Staatliche_Sammlung_f%C3%BCr_%C3%84gyptische_Kunst "Staatliche Sammlung für Ägyptische Kunst") (the State Collection of Egyptian Art).[[160]](#cite_note-163)[[161]](#cite_note-164)[[162]](#cite_note-165)

Several public collections of [LMU Munich](/wiki/LMU_Munich "LMU Munich") are still housed in the [Kunstareal](/wiki/Kunstareal "Kunstareal"). The expanded state collections are housed in the [Paläontologisches Museum München](/wiki/Pal%C3%A4ontologisches_Museum_M%C3%BCnchen "Paläontologisches Museum München"), and the [Zoologische Staatssammlung München](/wiki/Zoologische_Staatssammlung_M%C3%BCnchen "Zoologische Staatssammlung München").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] After the first German art exhibition in the [Glaspalast](/wiki/Glaspalast_(Munich) "Glaspalast (Munich)") for an international audience in 1869, Munich emerged as a focal point for the arts. Men of distinction from around the world visited the [Academy of Fine Arts](/wiki/Academy_of_Fine_Arts,_Munich "Academy of Fine Arts, Munich") under the directorship of [Karl von Piloty](/wiki/Karl_von_Piloty "Karl von Piloty") and later [Wilhelm von Kaulbach](/wiki/Wilhelm_von_Kaulbach "Wilhelm von Kaulbach").[[163]](#cite_note-166)

The [Museum Five Continents](/wiki/Museum_Five_Continents "Museum Five Continents") is the second largest collection in Germany of artefacts and objects from outside Europe, while the [Bavarian National Museum](/wiki/Bavarian_National_Museum "Bavarian National Museum") and the adjoining [Bavarian State Archaeological Collection](/wiki/Bavarian_State_Archaeological_Collection "Bavarian State Archaeological Collection") display regional art and cultural history. The [Schackgalerie](/wiki/Schackgalerie "Schackgalerie") is an important gallery of German 19th-century paintings.[[164]](#cite_note-167)

The memorial museum of the former [Dachau concentration camp](/wiki/Dachau_concentration_camp "Dachau concentration camp") is just outside the city.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Nationaltheater_Munich.jpg/250px-Nationaltheater_Munich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nationaltheater_Munich.jpg)

National Theatre

Munich is a major international musical centre and has played host to many prominent composers including [Orlande de Lassus](/wiki/Orlande_de_Lassus "Orlande de Lassus"), [Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart](/wiki/Wolfgang_Amadeus_Mozart "Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart"), [Carl Maria von Weber](/wiki/Carl_Maria_von_Weber "Carl Maria von Weber"), [Richard Wagner](/wiki/Richard_Wagner "Richard Wagner"), [Gustav Mahler](/wiki/Gustav_Mahler "Gustav Mahler"), [Richard Strauss](/wiki/Richard_Strauss "Richard Strauss"), [Max Reger](/wiki/Max_Reger "Max Reger") and [Carl Orff](/wiki/Carl_Orff "Carl Orff"). Some of classical music's best-known compositions have been created in and around Munich by composers born in the area, for example, Richard Strauss's tone poem *[Also sprach Zarathustra](/wiki/Also_sprach_Zarathustra_(Strauss) "Also sprach Zarathustra (Strauss)")* or Carl Orff's *[Carmina Burana](/wiki/Carmina_Burana "Carmina Burana")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[165]](#cite_note-168)

### Opera

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Opera")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Gasteig_Philharmonie_14.jpg/250px-Gasteig_Philharmonie_14.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gasteig_Philharmonie_14.jpg)

Gasteig

[Richard Wagner](/wiki/Richard_Wagner "Richard Wagner") was a supporter of [William I, German Emperor](/wiki/William_I,_German_Emperor "William I, German Emperor"), but Wagner only found a generous patron in [Ludwig II of Bavaria](/wiki/Ludwig_II_of_Bavaria "Ludwig II of Bavaria").[[166]](#cite_note-169) 1870 til 1871 Wagner premiered *[Die Meistersinger von Nürnberg](/wiki/Die_Meistersinger_von_N%C3%BCrnberg "Die Meistersinger von Nürnberg")* (*The Mastersingers of Nuremberg*) in Munich, a popular success for Wagner and King Ludwig II. Wagner premiered at the Hoftheater, now the [National Theatre Munich](/wiki/National_Theatre_Munich "National Theatre Munich"), with [Angelo Quaglio the Younger](/wiki/Angelo_Quaglio_the_Younger "Angelo Quaglio the Younger") designing the premiere production.[[167]](#cite_note-170)

The National Theatre Munich is now the home of the [Bavarian State Opera](/wiki/Bavarian_State_Opera "Bavarian State Opera") and the [Bavarian State Orchestra](/wiki/Bavarian_State_Orchestra "Bavarian State Orchestra"). Next door, the modern [Residenz Theatre](/wiki/Residenz_Theatre "Residenz Theatre") was erected in the building that also houses the [Cuvilliés Theatre](/wiki/Cuvilli%C3%A9s_Theatre "Cuvilliés Theatre"). The [Staatstheater am Gärtnerplatz](/wiki/Staatstheater_am_G%C3%A4rtnerplatz "Staatstheater am Gärtnerplatz") is a state theater while another opera house, the [Prinzregententheater](/wiki/Prinzregententheater "Prinzregententheater"), has become the home of the Bavarian Theater Academy and the [Munich Chamber Orchestra](/wiki/Munich_Chamber_Orchestra "Munich Chamber Orchestra").

### Orchestra

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Orchestra")]

The modern [Gasteig](/wiki/Gasteig "Gasteig") centre houses the [Munich Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Munich_Philharmonic "Munich Philharmonic"). The third orchestra in Munich with international importance is the [Bavarian Radio Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Bavarian_Radio_Symphony_Orchestra "Bavarian Radio Symphony Orchestra"). Its primary concert venue is the Herkulessaal in the former city royal residence, the [Munich Residenz](/wiki/Munich_Residenz "Munich Residenz"). Many important conductors have been attracted by the city's orchestras, including [Felix Weingartner](/wiki/Felix_Weingartner "Felix Weingartner"), [Hans Pfitzner](/wiki/Hans_Pfitzner "Hans Pfitzner"), [Hans Rosbaud](/wiki/Hans_Rosbaud "Hans Rosbaud"), [Hans Knappertsbusch](/wiki/Hans_Knappertsbusch "Hans Knappertsbusch"), [Sergiu Celibidache](/wiki/Sergiu_Celibidache "Sergiu Celibidache"), [James Levine](/wiki/James_Levine "James Levine"), [Christian Thielemann](/wiki/Christian_Thielemann "Christian Thielemann"), [Lorin Maazel](/wiki/Lorin_Maazel "Lorin Maazel"), [Rafael Kubelík](/wiki/Rafael_Kubel%C3%ADk "Rafael Kubelík"), [Eugen Jochum](/wiki/Eugen_Jochum "Eugen Jochum"), Sir [Colin Davis](/wiki/Colin_Davis "Colin Davis"), [Mariss Jansons](/wiki/Mariss_Jansons "Mariss Jansons"), [Bruno Walter](/wiki/Bruno_Walter "Bruno Walter"), [Georg Solti](/wiki/Georg_Solti "Georg Solti"), [Zubin Mehta](/wiki/Zubin_Mehta "Zubin Mehta") and [Kent Nagano](/wiki/Kent_Nagano "Kent Nagano"). A stage for shows, big events and musicals is the [Deutsche Theater](/wiki/Deutsches_Theater_M%C3%BCnchen "Deutsches Theater München"). It is Germany's largest theatre for guest performances.[[168]](#cite_note-171)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/FriedensengelMunchen.JPG/250px-FriedensengelMunchen.JPG)](/wiki/File:FriedensengelMunchen.JPG)

The Golden Friedensengel

### Pop and electronica

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Pop and electronica")]

Munich was the centre of [Krautrock](/wiki/Krautrock "Krautrock") in southern Germany, with many important bands such as [Amon Düül II](/wiki/Amon_D%C3%BC%C3%BCl_II "Amon Düül II"), [Embryo](/wiki/Embryo_(band) "Embryo (band)") or [Popol Vuh](/wiki/Popol_Vuh_(band) "Popol Vuh (band)") hailing from the city. In the 1970s, the [Musicland Studios](/wiki/Musicland_Studios "Musicland Studios") developed into one of the most prominent recording studios in the world, with bands such as the [Rolling Stones](/wiki/Rolling_Stones "Rolling Stones"), [Led Zeppelin](/wiki/Led_Zeppelin "Led Zeppelin"), [Deep Purple](/wiki/Deep_Purple "Deep Purple") and [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)") recording albums there. Munich also played a significant role in the development of electronic music, with genre pioneer [Giorgio Moroder](/wiki/Giorgio_Moroder "Giorgio Moroder"), who invented [synth](/wiki/Synth "Synth") [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco") and [electronic dance music](/wiki/Electronic_dance_music "Electronic dance music"), and [Donna Summer](/wiki/Donna_Summer "Donna Summer"), one of disco music's most important performers, both living and working in the city. In the late 1990s, [Electroclash](/wiki/Electroclash "Electroclash") was substantially co-invented if not even invented in Munich, when [DJ Hell](/wiki/DJ_Hell "DJ Hell") introduced and assembled international pioneers of this musical genre through his [International DeeJay Gigolo Records](/wiki/International_DeeJay_Gigolo_Records "International DeeJay Gigolo Records") label here.[[169]](#cite_note-mjunikdisco-172)

Other notable musicians and bands from Munich include [Konstantin Wecker](/wiki/Konstantin_Wecker "Konstantin Wecker"), [Willy Astor](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Willy_Astor "de:Willy Astor"), [Spider Murphy Gang](/wiki/Spider_Murphy_Gang "Spider Murphy Gang"), [Münchener Freiheit](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchener_Freiheit_(band) "Münchener Freiheit (band)"), [Lou Bega](/wiki/Lou_Bega "Lou Bega"), [Megaherz](/wiki/Megaherz "Megaherz"), [FSK](/wiki/FSK_(band) "FSK (band)"), [Colour Haze](/wiki/Colour_Haze "Colour Haze") and [Sportfreunde Stiller](/wiki/Sportfreunde_Stiller "Sportfreunde Stiller").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Munich hosted several *Love Parades* and *Mayday Party* [rave](/wiki/Rave "Rave") events throughout the 1990s. Munich continues to rave, the local youth scenes are active.[[170]](#cite_note-173)

### Theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Theatre")]

The [Munich Kammerspiele](/wiki/Munich_Kammerspiele "Munich Kammerspiele") is one of the most important German-language theaters. Since [Gotthold Ephraim Lessing](/wiki/Gotthold_Ephraim_Lessing "Gotthold Ephraim Lessing")'s premieres in 1775 many important writers have staged their plays in Munich, they include [Christian Friedrich Hebbel](/wiki/Christian_Friedrich_Hebbel "Christian Friedrich Hebbel"), [Henrik Ibsen](/wiki/Henrik_Ibsen "Henrik Ibsen"), and [Hugo von Hofmannsthal](/wiki/Hugo_von_Hofmannsthal "Hugo von Hofmannsthal").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Schwabing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Schwabing")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Vassily_Kandinsky%2C_1908_-_Houses_in_Munich.jpg/250px-Vassily_Kandinsky%2C_1908_-_Houses_in_Munich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vassily_Kandinsky,_1908_-_Houses_in_Munich.jpg)

Wassily Kandinsky's *Houses in Munich* (1908)

At the turn of the 20th century [Schwabing](/wiki/Schwabing "Schwabing") was a preeminent cultural metropolis. Schwabing was an epicenter for both literature and the fine arts, with numerous German and non-German artists living there.[[171]](#cite_note-174)

[Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") authored *[What Is to Be Done?](/wiki/What_Is_to_Be_Done%3F "What Is to Be Done?")* while living in Schwabing. Central to Schwabing's bohemian scene were *Künstlerlokale* (*Artist's Cafés*) like [Café Stefanie](/wiki/Caf%C3%A9_Stefanie "Café Stefanie") or Kabarett [Simpl](/wiki/Simpl_(Munich) "Simpl (Munich)"), whose liberal ways differed fundamentally from Munich's more traditional localities. The Simpl, which survives to this day, was named after Munich's anti-authoritarian satirical magazine *[Simplicissimus](/wiki/Simplicissimus "Simplicissimus")*, founded in 1896 by [Albert Langen](/wiki/Albert_Langen "Albert Langen") and [Thomas Theodor Heine](/wiki/Thomas_Theodor_Heine "Thomas Theodor Heine"), which quickly became an important organ of the *Schwabinger Bohème*. Its caricatures and biting satirical attacks on [Wilhelmine](/wiki/Wilhelminism "Wilhelminism") German society were the result of countless of collaborative efforts by many of the best visual artists and writers from Munich and elsewhere.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1971 [Eckart Witzigmann](/wiki/Eckart_Witzigmann "Eckart Witzigmann") teamed up with a Munich building contractor to finance and open the *Tantris* restaurant in Schwabing. Witzigmann is credited for starting the German *Küchenwunder* (*kitchen wonder*).[[172]](#cite_note-175)

### Biedermeier

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Biedermeier")]

The [Biedermeier](/wiki/Biedermeier "Biedermeier") era was named after a character that regularly appeared in the satire magazine *Münchner Fliegende Blätter* (*Loose Munich Pages*), which was published by [Adolf Kussmaul](/wiki/Adolf_Kussmaul "Adolf Kussmaul") and [Ludwig Eichrodt](/wiki/Ludwig_Eichrodt "Ludwig Eichrodt") in Munich between 1855 and 1857. Biedermeier was a synonym for arts, furniture, and the lifestyle of the nonheroic middle class. The Biedermeier era painters [Ferdinand Georg Waldmüller](/wiki/Ferdinand_Georg_Waldm%C3%BCller "Ferdinand Georg Waldmüller"), [Moritz von Schwind](/wiki/Moritz_von_Schwind "Moritz von Schwind"), and [Carl Spitzweg](/wiki/Carl_Spitzweg "Carl Spitzweg") are shown in the [Neue Pinakothek](/wiki/Neue_Pinakothek "Neue Pinakothek").[[173]](#cite_note-176)

### Prinzregentenzeit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Prinzregentenzeit")]

Celebrity literary figures worked in Munich especially during the final decades of the Kingdom of Bavaria, the so-called *Prinzregentenzeit* (literally *prince regent's time*) under the reign of [Luitpold, Prince Regent of Bavaria](/wiki/Luitpold,_Prince_Regent_of_Bavaria "Luitpold, Prince Regent of Bavaria"). This includes [Thomas Mann](/wiki/Thomas_Mann "Thomas Mann"), [Heinrich Mann](/wiki/Heinrich_Mann "Heinrich Mann"), [Paul Johann Ludwig von Heyse](/wiki/Paul_Johann_Ludwig_von_Heyse "Paul Johann Ludwig von Heyse"), [Rainer Maria Rilke](/wiki/Rainer_Maria_Rilke "Rainer Maria Rilke"), [Ludwig Thoma](/wiki/Ludwig_Thoma "Ludwig Thoma"), [Fanny zu Reventlow](/wiki/Fanny_zu_Reventlow "Fanny zu Reventlow"), [Oskar Panizza](/wiki/Oskar_Panizza "Oskar Panizza"), [Gustav Meyrink](/wiki/Gustav_Meyrink "Gustav Meyrink"), [Max Halbe](/wiki/Max_Halbe "Max Halbe"), [Erich Mühsam](/wiki/Erich_M%C3%BChsam "Erich Mühsam") and [Frank Wedekind](/wiki/Frank_Wedekind "Frank Wedekind").

### Weimar Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Weimar Republic")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Schrimpf_oskar_maria_graf.png/250px-Schrimpf_oskar_maria_graf.png)](/wiki/File:Schrimpf_oskar_maria_graf.png)

Portrait of Oskar Maria Graf by [Georg Schrimpf](/wiki/Georg_Schrimpf "Georg Schrimpf") (1927)

The period immediately before World War I saw continued economic and cultural prominence for the city. [Thomas Mann](/wiki/Thomas_Mann "Thomas Mann") wrote in his novella *Gladius Dei* about this period: "München leuchtete" (literally "Munich shone"). Munich remained a centre of cultural life during the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic"), with figures such as [Lion Feuchtwanger](/wiki/Lion_Feuchtwanger "Lion Feuchtwanger"), [Bertolt Brecht](/wiki/Bertolt_Brecht "Bertolt Brecht"), [Peter Paul Althaus](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Althaus "Peter Paul Althaus"), [Stefan George](/wiki/Stefan_George "Stefan George"), [Ricarda Huch](/wiki/Ricarda_Huch "Ricarda Huch"), [Joachim Ringelnatz](/wiki/Joachim_Ringelnatz "Joachim Ringelnatz"), [Oskar Maria Graf](/wiki/Oskar_Maria_Graf "Oskar Maria Graf"), [Annette Kolb](/wiki/Annette_Kolb "Annette Kolb"), [Ernst Toller](/wiki/Ernst_Toller "Ernst Toller"), [Hugo Ball](/wiki/Hugo_Ball "Hugo Ball"), and [Klaus Mann](/wiki/Klaus_Mann "Klaus Mann") adding to the already established big names.[[174]](#cite_note-177)

[Karl Valentin](/wiki/Karl_Valentin "Karl Valentin"), the cabaret performer and comedian, is to this day remembered and beloved as a cultural icon of his hometown. Between 1910 and 1940, he wrote and performed in many absurdist sketches and short films that were highly influential, earning him the nickname of "[Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") of Germany".[[175]](#cite_note-178)[[176]](#cite_note-179)

[Liesl Karlstadt](/wiki/Liesl_Karlstadt "Liesl Karlstadt"), before working together with Valentin, cross-dressed and performed cabaret with [yodeling](/wiki/Yodeling "Yodeling") on stage and in Munich's Cafe-Theatres. The cabaret scene was crushed when the Nazis seized power in 1933 and Karlstadt was saved from Nazi sterilization by a doctor. Contemporary Munich cabaret still reverences 1920s cabaret, the Munich alternative rock band [F.S.K.](/wiki/F.S.K._(band) "F.S.K. (band)") absorbs yodels.[[177]](#cite_note-180)

### Post-war literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Post-war literature")]

After World War II, Munich soon again became a focal point of the German literary scene and remains so to this day, with writers as diverse as [Wolfgang Koeppen](/wiki/Wolfgang_Koeppen "Wolfgang Koeppen"), [Erich Kästner](/wiki/Erich_K%C3%A4stner "Erich Kästner"), [Eugen Roth](/wiki/Eugen_Roth "Eugen Roth"), [Alfred Andersch](/wiki/Alfred_Andersch "Alfred Andersch"), [Elfriede Jelinek](/wiki/Elfriede_Jelinek "Elfriede Jelinek"), [Hans Magnus Enzensberger](/wiki/Hans_Magnus_Enzensberger "Hans Magnus Enzensberger"), [Michael Ende](/wiki/Michael_Ende "Michael Ende"), [Franz Xaver Kroetz](/wiki/Franz_Xaver_Kroetz "Franz Xaver Kroetz"), [Gerhard Polt](/wiki/Gerhard_Polt "Gerhard Polt") and [Patrick Süskind](/wiki/Patrick_S%C3%BCskind "Patrick Süskind") calling the city their home.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Fine arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Fine arts")]

From the Gothic to the Baroque era, the fine arts were represented in Munich by artists like [Erasmus Grasser](/wiki/Erasmus_Grasser "Erasmus Grasser"), [Jan Polack](/wiki/Jan_Polack "Jan Polack"), [Johann Baptist Straub](/wiki/Johann_Baptist_Straub "Johann Baptist Straub"), [Ignaz Günther](/wiki/Ignaz_G%C3%BCnther "Ignaz Günther"), [Hans Krumpper](/wiki/Hans_Krumpper "Hans Krumpper"), [Ludwig von Schwanthaler](/wiki/Ludwig_Michael_Schwanthaler "Ludwig Michael Schwanthaler"), [Cosmas Damian Asam](/wiki/Cosmas_Damian_Asam "Cosmas Damian Asam"), [Egid Quirin Asam](/wiki/Egid_Quirin_Asam "Egid Quirin Asam"), [Johann Baptist Zimmermann](/wiki/Johann_Baptist_Zimmermann "Johann Baptist Zimmermann"), [Johann Michael Fischer](/wiki/Johann_Michael_Fischer "Johann Michael Fischer") and [François de Cuvilliés](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Cuvilli%C3%A9s "François de Cuvilliés"). Munich had already become an important place for painters like [Carl Rottmann](/wiki/Carl_Rottmann "Carl Rottmann"), [Lovis Corinth](/wiki/Lovis_Corinth "Lovis Corinth"), [Wilhelm von Kaulbach](/wiki/Wilhelm_von_Kaulbach "Wilhelm von Kaulbach"), [Carl Spitzweg](/wiki/Carl_Spitzweg "Carl Spitzweg"), [Franz von Lenbach](/wiki/Franz_von_Lenbach "Franz von Lenbach"), [Franz Stuck](/wiki/Franz_Stuck "Franz Stuck"), [Karl Piloty](/wiki/Karl_Piloty "Karl Piloty") and [Wilhelm Leibl](/wiki/Wilhelm_Leibl "Wilhelm Leibl").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Cinema

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Cinema")]

Munich was (and in some cases, still is) home to many of the most important authors of the [New German Cinema](/wiki/New_German_Cinema "New German Cinema") movement, including [Rainer Werner Fassbinder](/wiki/Rainer_Werner_Fassbinder "Rainer Werner Fassbinder"), [Werner Herzog](/wiki/Werner_Herzog "Werner Herzog"), [Edgar Reitz](/wiki/Edgar_Reitz "Edgar Reitz") and [Herbert Achternbusch](/wiki/Herbert_Achternbusch "Herbert Achternbusch"). In 1971, the [Filmverlag der Autoren](/wiki/Filmverlag_der_Autoren "Filmverlag der Autoren") was founded, cementing the city's role in the movement's history. Munich served as the location for many of Fassbinder's films, among them *[Ali: Fear Eats the Soul](/wiki/Ali:_Fear_Eats_the_Soul "Ali: Fear Eats the Soul")*. The Hotel [Deutsche Eiche](/w/index.php?title=Deutsche_Eiche&action=edit&redlink=1 "Deutsche Eiche (page does not exist)") near Gärtnerplatz was somewhat like a centre of operations for Fassbinder and his "clan" of actors. New German Cinema is considered by far the most important artistic movement in German cinema history since the era of [German Expressionism](/wiki/German_Expressionism "German Expressionism") in the 1920s.[[178]](#cite_note-181)[[179]](#cite_note-182)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Bavaria_Film-_und_Fernsehstudios_logo.svg/250px-Bavaria_Film-_und_Fernsehstudios_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bavaria_Film-_und_Fernsehstudios_logo.svg)

Logo of [Bavaria Film](/wiki/Bavaria_Film "Bavaria Film")

In 1919, the [Bavaria Film Studios](/wiki/Bavaria_Studios "Bavaria Studios") were founded, which developed into one of Europe's largest film studios. Directors like [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock"), [Billy Wilder](/wiki/Billy_Wilder "Billy Wilder"), [Orson Welles](/wiki/Orson_Welles "Orson Welles"), [John Huston](/wiki/John_Huston "John Huston"), [Ingmar Bergman](/wiki/Ingmar_Bergman "Ingmar Bergman"), [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick"), [Claude Chabrol](/wiki/Claude_Chabrol "Claude Chabrol"), [Fritz Umgelter](/wiki/Fritz_Umgelter "Fritz Umgelter"), [Rainer Werner Fassbinder](/wiki/Rainer_Werner_Fassbinder "Rainer Werner Fassbinder"), [Wolfgang Petersen](/wiki/Wolfgang_Petersen "Wolfgang Petersen") and [Wim Wenders](/wiki/Wim_Wenders "Wim Wenders") made films there. Among the internationally well-known films produced at the studios are *[The Pleasure Garden](/wiki/The_Pleasure_Garden_(1925_film) "The Pleasure Garden (1925 film)")* (1925) by Alfred Hitchcock, *[The Great Escape](/wiki/The_Great_Escape_(film) "The Great Escape (film)")* (1963) by [John Sturges](/wiki/John_Sturges "John Sturges"), *[Paths of Glory](/wiki/Paths_of_Glory "Paths of Glory")* (1957) by Stanley Kubrick, *[Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory](/wiki/Willy_Wonka_%26_the_Chocolate_Factory "Willy Wonka & the Chocolate Factory")* (1971) by [Mel Stuart](/wiki/Mel_Stuart "Mel Stuart") and both *[Das Boot](/wiki/Das_Boot "Das Boot")* (1981) and *[The Neverending Story](/wiki/The_Neverending_Story_(film) "The Neverending Story (film)")* (1984) by [Wolfgang Petersen](/wiki/Wolfgang_Petersen "Wolfgang Petersen"). Munich remains one of the centres of the German film and entertainment industry.[[180]](#cite_note-183)

### Festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Festivals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Hippodrom_Zelt_Oktoberfest.jpg/250px-Hippodrom_Zelt_Oktoberfest.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hippodrom_Zelt_Oktoberfest.jpg)

[Oktoberfest](/wiki/Oktoberfest "Oktoberfest")

#### Coopers' Dance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Coopers' Dance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Sch%C3%A4fflertanz_2012-05.JPG/250px-Sch%C3%A4fflertanz_2012-05.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sch%C3%A4fflertanz_2012-05.JPG)

Schäfflertanz in [Neuhausen](/wiki/Neuhausen-Nymphenburg "Neuhausen-Nymphenburg"), 2012

The [Coopers' Dance](/wiki/Coopers%27_Dance "Coopers' Dance") (German: *Schäfflertanz*) is a [guild](/wiki/Medieval_guild "Medieval guild") dance of [coopers](/wiki/Cooper_(profession) "Cooper (profession)") originally started in Munich. Beginning in the early 1800s the custom spread via the travels of [journeymen](/wiki/Journeyman "Journeyman"), and it is now a common tradition throughout the [Old Bavaria](/wiki/Old_Bavaria "Old Bavaria") region. The dance was supposed to be held every seven years.[[181]](#cite_note-myth-184)

#### Starkbierfest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Starkbierfest")]

March and April, for three weeks during [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), celebrating Munich's "strong beer". Starkbier was created in 1651 by the local [Paulinerkirche, Leipzig](/wiki/Paulinerkirche,_Leipzig "Paulinerkirche, Leipzig") [monks](/wiki/Monk "Monk") who drank this 'Flüssiges Brot', or 'liquid bread'. It became a public festival in 1751 and is now the second largest beer festival in Munich. A Starkbierfest celebration may be held in [beer halls](/wiki/Beer_hall "Beer hall") and pubs.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Frühlingsfest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Frühlingsfest")]

Held for two weeks at the [Theresienwiese](/wiki/Theresienwiese "Theresienwiese") from the end of April to the beginning of May, celebrating and serving the new local spring beers.[[182]](#cite_note-185)

#### Auer Dult

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Auer Dult")]

Main article: [Auer Dult](/wiki/Auer_Dult "Auer Dult")

A regular event combining a [market](/wiki/Marketplace "Marketplace") and a German style [folk festival](/wiki/Volksfest "Volksfest") on the [Mariahilfplatz](/wiki/Mariahilfplatz "Mariahilfplatz"). The Auer Dult can be up to 300 stalls, selling handmade crafts, [household goods](/wiki/Household_goods "Household goods"), and [local foods](/wiki/Local_food "Local food").[[183]](#cite_note-186)

#### Kocherlball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Kocherlball")]

Munich's Kocherlball (*Cooks' Ball*) is an annual event to commemorate all servants, ranging from kitchenhands to cooks. The tradition started in the 19th century.[[184]](#cite_note-187)

#### Tollwood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Tollwood")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Tollwood_Winterfestival_Munich_2010_b.jpg/250px-Tollwood_Winterfestival_Munich_2010_b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tollwood_Winterfestival_Munich_2010_b.jpg)

Tollwood Winterfestival

Usually held annually in July and December at Olympia Park. The [Tollwood Festival](/wiki/Tollwood_Festival "Tollwood Festival") showcases fine and performing arts with live music, and several lanes of booths selling handmade crafts, as well as [organic food](/wiki/Organic_food "Organic food"), mostly [fusion cuisine](/wiki/Fusion_cuisine "Fusion cuisine").[[185]](#cite_note-188)

#### Oktoberfest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Oktoberfest")]

At [Theresienwiese](/wiki/Theresienwiese "Theresienwiese"), Munich's [Oktoberfest](/wiki/Oktoberfest "Oktoberfest"), the largest [beer festival](/wiki/Beer_festival "Beer festival") in the world, runs for 16–18 days from the end of September through early October. In the last 200 years the festival has grown to span 85 acres and now welcomes over six million visitors every year. Beer is served from the six major Munich [breweries](/wiki/Brewery "Brewery"): [Augustiner-Bräu](/wiki/Augustiner-Br%C3%A4u "Augustiner-Bräu"), [Hacker-Pschorr Brewery](/wiki/Hacker-Pschorr_Brewery "Hacker-Pschorr Brewery"), [Löwenbräu Brewery](/wiki/L%C3%B6wenbr%C3%A4u_Brewery "Löwenbräu Brewery"), [Paulaner Brewery](/wiki/Paulaner_Brewery "Paulaner Brewery"), [Spaten-Franziskaner-Bräu](/wiki/Spaten-Franziskaner-Br%C3%A4u "Spaten-Franziskaner-Bräu"), and [Staatliches Hofbräuhaus in München](/wiki/Staatliches_Hofbr%C3%A4uhaus_in_M%C3%BCnchen "Staatliches Hofbräuhaus in München").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Food must be bought in each tent.[[186]](#cite_note-189)

#### Christkindlmarkt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: Christkindlmarkt")]

The Munich [Christkindlmarkt](/wiki/Christkindlmarkt "Christkindlmarkt") started to evolve in the 14th century. The German Christkindlmarkt reached the desired accomplishment[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] in the 17th century in [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg").[[187]](#cite_note-190)

### Cuisine and culinary specialities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: Cuisine and culinary specialities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Weisswurst.jpg/250px-Weisswurst.jpg)](/wiki/File:Weisswurst.jpg)

Weißwurst with sweet mustard and a pretzel

Munich cuisine is a part of the broader [Bavarian cuisine](/wiki/Bavarian_cuisine "Bavarian cuisine"). Munich [Weißwurst](/wiki/Wei%C3%9Fwurst "Weißwurst") ("white sausage", *German: Münchner Weißwurst*) was invented in the city in 1857 and is a Munich speciality. Traditionally Weißwurst is served in pubs before noon and is served with [sweet mustard](/wiki/Sweet_mustard "Sweet mustard") and freshly baked [pretzels](/wiki/Pretzel "Pretzel").

Munich has 11 restaurants that have been awarded one or more [Michelin Guide](/wiki/Michelin_Guide "Michelin Guide") stars in 2021.[[188]](#cite_note-MichelinGuide-2021-191)

### Beers and breweries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Beers and breweries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/BIER_IM_EG.jpg/250px-BIER_IM_EG.jpg)](/wiki/File:BIER_IM_EG.jpg)

Helles beer

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/MUC_Westend_AugustinerbrauereiA.jpg/250px-MUC_Westend_AugustinerbrauereiA.jpg)](/wiki/File:MUC_Westend_AugustinerbrauereiA.jpg)

[Augustiner brewery](/wiki/Augustiner-Br%C3%A4u "Augustiner-Bräu")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Biergarten_at_Night_2.JPG/250px-Biergarten_at_Night_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Biergarten_at_Night_2.JPG)

Beer garden in Munich

Munich is known for its breweries and [Weissbier](/wiki/Weissbier "Weissbier") (*wheat beer*). [Helles](/wiki/Helles "Helles"), a [pale lager](/wiki/Pale_lager "Pale lager") with a translucent gold color, is the most popular contemporary Munich beer. Helles has largely replaced Munich's dark beer, known as [Dunkel](/wiki/Dunkel "Dunkel"), which gets its color from roasted malt. It was the typical beer in Munich in the 19th century. Starkbier is the strongest Munich beer, with a high alcohol content of 6%–9%. It is dark amber in color and has a heavy malty taste. The beer served at [Oktoberfest](/wiki/Oktoberfest "Oktoberfest") is a special type of beer with a higher alcohol content.

Wirtshäuser are traditional Bavarian pubs, many of which also have small outside areas. Biergärten (*[beer gardens](/wiki/Beer_garden "Beer garden")*) are a popular fixture in Munich's gastronomic landscape. They are central to the city's culture, and are an overt melting pot for members of all walks of life, regardless of social class. There are many smaller beer gardens, but some beer gardens have thousands of seats. Large beer gardens can be found in the [Englischer Garten](/wiki/Englischer_Garten "Englischer Garten"), on the Nockherberg, and in the Hirschgarten.

The six main breweries in Munich are [Augustiner-Bräu](/wiki/Augustiner-Br%C3%A4u "Augustiner-Bräu"), [Hacker-Pschorr Brewery](/wiki/Hacker-Pschorr_Brewery "Hacker-Pschorr Brewery"), [Hofbräuhaus](/wiki/Hofbr%C3%A4uhaus "Hofbräuhaus"), [Löwenbräu](/wiki/L%C3%B6wenbr%C3%A4u "Löwenbräu"), [Paulaner](/wiki/Paulaner "Paulaner"), and [Spaten-Franziskaner-Bräu](/wiki/Spaten-Franziskaner-Br%C3%A4u "Spaten-Franziskaner-Bräu"). Smaller breweries are becoming more prevalent in Munich.

### Circus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: Circus")]

The [Circus Krone](/wiki/Circus_Krone "Circus Krone") based in Munich is one of the largest circuses in Europe.[[189]](#cite_note-192) It was the first and still is one of only a few in Western Europe to also occupy a [building](/wiki/Circus_Krone_Building "Circus Krone Building") of its own.

### Nightlife

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=65 "Edit section: Nightlife")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Alte_Utting_6144.jpg/250px-Alte_Utting_6144.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alte_Utting_6144.jpg)

The party ship [Alte Utting](/wiki/Alte_Utting "Alte Utting")

[Nightlife](/wiki/Nightlife "Nightlife") in Munich is located mostly in the boroughs [Ludwigsvorstadt-Isarvorstadt](/wiki/Ludwigsvorstadt-Isarvorstadt "Ludwigsvorstadt-Isarvorstadt"), [Maxvorstadt](/wiki/Maxvorstadt "Maxvorstadt"), [Au-Haidhausen](/wiki/Au-Haidhausen "Au-Haidhausen"), [Berg am Laim](/wiki/Berg_am_Laim "Berg am Laim") and [Sendling](/wiki/Sendling "Sendling"). Between [Sendlinger Tor](/wiki/Sendlinger_Tor "Sendlinger Tor") and Maximiliansplatz, on the edge of the central [Altstadt-Lehel](/wiki/Altstadt-Lehel "Altstadt-Lehel") district, there is also the so-called Feierbanane (party banana), a roughly banana-shaped unofficial party zone spanning 1.3 km (0.8 mi) along Sonnenstraße, characterized by a high concentration of clubs, bars and restaurants, which became the center of Munich's nightlife in the mid-2000s.[[190]](#cite_note-sueddeutsche_20130706-193)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Bahnw%C3%A4rter_Thiel_Nightclub_Munich_Subway_Floor_1.jpg/250px-Bahnw%C3%A4rter_Thiel_Nightclub_Munich_Subway_Floor_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bahnw%C3%A4rter_Thiel_Nightclub_Munich_Subway_Floor_1.jpg)

[Bahnwärter Thiel](/wiki/Bahnw%C3%A4rter_Thiel_(club) "Bahnwärter Thiel (club)")

In the 1960s and 1970s, [Schwabing](/wiki/Schwabing "Schwabing") was considered a center of nightlife in Germany, with internationally known clubs such as [Big Apple](/wiki/Big_Apple_(club) "Big Apple (club)"), PN hit-house, Domicile, Hot Club, Piper Club, Tiffany, Germany's first large-scale discotheque [Blow Up](/wiki/Blow_Up_(club) "Blow Up (club)") and the underwater nightclub [Yellow Submarine](/wiki/Yellow_Submarine_(club) "Yellow Submarine (club)"),[[169]](#cite_note-mjunikdisco-172)[[191]](#cite_note-ertl-194)[[192]](#cite_note-mitvergnuegen_schauberger-195) and Munich has been called "New York's big disco sister" in this context.[[169]](#cite_note-mjunikdisco-172)[[193]](#cite_note-abendzeitung_20081121-196) Bars in the Schwabing district of this era include, among many others, [Schwabinger 7](/wiki/Schwabinger_7 "Schwabinger 7") and Schwabinger Podium. Since the 1980s, however, Schwabing has lost much of its nightlife activity due to [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") and the resulting high rents, and the formerly wild artists' and students' quarter developed into one of the city's most coveted and expensive residential districts, attracting affluent citizens with little interest in partying.[[194]](#cite_note-mitvergnuegen_nisslmüller-197)

Beginning in the 1960s, the Rosa Viertel (pink quarter) developed in the [Glockenbachviertel](/wiki/Ludwigsvorstadt-Isarvorstadt#Glockenbachviertel "Ludwigsvorstadt-Isarvorstadt") and around Gärtnerplatz, which in the 1980s made Munich "one of the four gayest metropolises in the world" along with San Francisco, New York City and Amsterdam.[[195]](#cite_note-stankiewitz-198) In particular, the area around [Müllerstraße](/wiki/M%C3%BCllerstra%C3%9Fe "Müllerstraße") and Hans-Sachs-Straße was characterized by numerous gay bars and nightclubs. One of them was the [travesty](/wiki/Travesti_(theatre) "Travesti (theatre)") nightclub Old Mrs. Henderson, where [Freddie Mercury](/wiki/Freddie_Mercury "Freddie Mercury"), who lived in Munich from 1979 to 1985, filmed the music video for the song "[Living on My Own](/wiki/Living_on_My_Own "Living on My Own")" at his 39th birthday party.[[195]](#cite_note-stankiewitz-198)[[192]](#cite_note-mitvergnuegen_schauberger-195)[[196]](#cite_note-BR_20211004-199)

Since the mid-1990s, the Kunstpark Ost and its successor Kultfabrik, a former industrial complex that was converted to a large party area near [München Ostbahnhof](/wiki/Munich_East_station "Munich East station") in [Berg am Laim](/wiki/Berg_am_Laim "Berg am Laim"), hosted more than 30 clubs and was especially popular among younger people from the metropolitan area surrounding Munich and tourists.[[195]](#cite_note-stankiewitz-198)[[197]](#cite_note-munichfoundcom-200) The Kultfabrik was closed at the end of the year 2015 to convert the area into a residential and office area. Apart from the Kultfarbik and the smaller Optimolwerke, there is a wide variety of establishments in the urban parts of nearby [Haidhausen](/wiki/Haidhausen_(Munich) "Haidhausen (Munich)"). Before the Kunstpark Ost, there had already been an accumulation of internationally known nightclubs in the remains of the abandoned former [Munich-Riem Airport](/wiki/Munich-Riem_Airport#Reuse "Munich-Riem Airport").[[169]](#cite_note-mjunikdisco-172)[[198]](#cite_note-mitvergnuegen_022017-201)[[199]](#cite_note-ard_072022-202)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Blitz_Club_Munich.jpg/250px-Blitz_Club_Munich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blitz_Club_Munich.jpg)

Blitz Club on Museumsinsel

Munich nightlife tends to change dramatically and quickly. Establishments open and close every year, and due to gentrification and the overheated housing market many survive only a few years, while others last longer. Beyond the already mentioned venues of the 1960s and 1970s, nightclubs with international recognition in recent history included Tanzlokal Größenwahn, [The Atomic Café](/wiki/The_Atomic_Caf%C3%A9_(club) "The Atomic Café (club)") and the techno clubs [Babalu Club](/wiki/Babalu_Club "Babalu Club"), [Ultraschall](/wiki/Ultraschall "Ultraschall"), [KW – Das Heizkraftwerk](/wiki/KW_%E2%80%93_Das_Heizkraftwerk "KW – Das Heizkraftwerk"), [Natraj Temple](/wiki/Natraj_Temple "Natraj Temple"), [MMA Club (Mixed Munich Arts)](/wiki/MMA_Club "MMA Club"), Die Registratur and Bob Beaman.[[200]](#cite_note-zeitmagazin-203) From 1995 to 2001, Munich was also home to the [Union Move](/wiki/Union_Move "Union Move"), one of the largest [technoparades](/wiki/Technoparade "Technoparade") in Germany.[[191]](#cite_note-ertl-194)

Munich has the highest density of music venues of any German city, followed by Hamburg, Cologne and Berlin.[[201]](#cite_note-fazemag_20210920-204)[[202]](#cite_note-clubstudy_2021-205) Within the city's limits are more than 100 nightclubs and thousands of bars and restaurants.[[203]](#cite_note-Munich_bars-206)[[204]](#cite_note-Munich_nightclubs-207)

Popular techno nightclubs are [Blitz Club](/wiki/Blitz_Club "Blitz Club"), Harry Klein, Rote Sonne, [Bahnwärter Thiel](/wiki/Bahnw%C3%A4rter_Thiel_(club) "Bahnwärter Thiel (club)"), Pimpernel, Charlie, Palais and Pathos.[[205]](#cite_note-sueddeutsche_20161016-208)[[206]](#cite_note-theclubmap-209) Popular mixed music clubs are Call me Drella, Wannda Circus, Tonhalle, Backstage, Muffathalle, Ampere, Pacha, P1, [Zenith](/wiki/Zenith_(building) "Zenith (building)"), Minna Thiel and the party ship [Alte Utting](/wiki/Alte_Utting "Alte Utting").

Munich is a leading location for science and research with a long list of Nobel Prize laureates from [Wilhelm Röntgen](/wiki/Wilhelm_R%C3%B6ntgen "Wilhelm Röntgen") in 1901 to [Theodor W. Hänsch](/wiki/Theodor_W._H%C3%A4nsch "Theodor W. Hänsch") in 2005.

LMU Munich[[207]](#cite_note-210) and the Technical University of Munich (TUM),[[208]](#cite_note-211) were two of the first three German universities to be awarded the title *elite university* by a selection committee composed of academics and members of the Ministries of Education and Research of the Federation and the German states (Länder).

* [LMU Munich](/wiki/LMU_Munich "LMU Munich")), founded in 1472 in [Ingolstadt](/wiki/Ingolstadt "Ingolstadt"), moved to Munich in 1826
* [Technical University of Munich](/wiki/Technical_University_of_Munich "Technical University of Munich") (TUM), founded in 1868
* [Akademie der Bildenden Künste München](/wiki/Academy_of_Fine_Arts,_Munich "Academy of Fine Arts, Munich"), founded in 1808
* [Bundeswehr University Munich](/wiki/Bundeswehr_University_Munich "Bundeswehr University Munich"), founded in 1973 (located in [Neubiberg](/wiki/Neubiberg "Neubiberg"))
* [Deutsche Journalistenschule](/wiki/Deutsche_Journalistenschule "Deutsche Journalistenschule"), founded in 1959
* [Bayerische Akademie für Außenwirtschaft](/wiki/Bayerische_Akademie_f%C3%BCr_Au%C3%9Fenwirtschaft "Bayerische Akademie für Außenwirtschaft"), founded in 1989
* [Hochschule für Musik und Theater München](/wiki/Hochschule_f%C3%BCr_Musik_und_Theater_M%C3%BCnchen "Hochschule für Musik und Theater München"), founded in 1830
* [International Max Planck Research School for Molecular and Cellular Life Sciences](/wiki/International_Max_Planck_Research_School_for_Molecular_and_Cellular_Life_Sciences "International Max Planck Research School for Molecular and Cellular Life Sciences"), founded in 2005
* [International School of Management, Germany](/wiki/International_School_of_Management,_Germany "International School of Management, Germany"), founded in 1990
* [Katholische Stiftungsfachhochschule München](/w/index.php?title=Katholische_Stiftungsfachhochschule_M%C3%BCnchen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Katholische Stiftungsfachhochschule München (page does not exist)"), founded in 1971
* [Munich Business School](/wiki/Munich_Business_School "Munich Business School") (MBS), founded in 1991
* [Munich Intellectual Property Law Center](/wiki/Munich_Intellectual_Property_Law_Center "Munich Intellectual Property Law Center") (MIPLC), founded in 2003
* [Munich School of Philosophy](/wiki/Munich_School_of_Philosophy "Munich School of Philosophy"), founded in 1925 in [Pullach](/wiki/Pullach "Pullach"), moved to Munich in 1971
* [Munich School of Political Science](/wiki/Munich_School_of_Political_Science "Munich School of Political Science"), founded in 1950
* [Munich University of Applied Sciences](/wiki/Munich_University_of_Applied_Sciences "Munich University of Applied Sciences") (HM), founded in 1971
* [New European College](/wiki/New_European_College "New European College"), founded in 2014
* [Ukrainian Free University](/wiki/Ukrainian_Free_University "Ukrainian Free University"), founded in 1921 (from 1945 – in Munich)
* [University of Television and Film Munich](/wiki/University_of_Television_and_Film_Munich "University of Television and Film Munich") (*Hochschule für Fernsehen und Film*), founded in 1966

### Primary and secondary schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=68 "Edit section: Primary and secondary schools")]

Notable Gymnasien in Munich include the [Maria-Theresia-Gymnasium](/wiki/Maria-Theresia-Gymnasium "Maria-Theresia-Gymnasium"), the [Luitpold Gymnasium](/wiki/Luitpold_Gymnasium "Luitpold Gymnasium"), the [Wilhelmsgymnasium](/wiki/Wilhelmsgymnasium_(Munich) "Wilhelmsgymnasium (Munich)"), as well as the [Wittelsbacher Gymnasium](/wiki/Wittelsbacher-Gymnasium_M%C3%BCnchen "Wittelsbacher-Gymnasium München"). Munich has several notable international schools, including [Lycée Jean Renoir](/wiki/Lyc%C3%A9e_Jean_Renoir_(Munich) "Lycée Jean Renoir (Munich)"), the [Japanische Internationale Schule München](/wiki/Japanische_Internationale_Schule_M%C3%BCnchen "Japanische Internationale Schule München"), the [Bavarian International School](/wiki/Bavarian_International_School "Bavarian International School"), the [Munich International School](/wiki/Munich_International_School "Munich International School"), and the [European School, Munich](/wiki/European_School,_Munich "European School, Munich").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=75 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/4_Cilindros%2C_M%C3%BAnich%2C_Alemania%2C_2013-02-11%2C_DD_02.JPG/250px-4_Cilindros%2C_M%C3%BAnich%2C_Alemania%2C_2013-02-11%2C_DD_02.JPG)](/wiki/File:4_Cilindros,_M%C3%BAnich,_Alemania,_2013-02-11,_DD_02.JPG)

[BMW Headquarters](/wiki/BMW_Headquarters "BMW Headquarters") building (one of the few buildings that has been built from the top to the bottom) and the bowl-shaped [BMW Museum](/wiki/BMW_Museum "BMW Museum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/BMW_Museum_Entrance.jpg/250px-BMW_Museum_Entrance.jpg)](/wiki/File:BMW_Museum_Entrance.jpg)

BMW Museum Entrance

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/SiemensForum.jpg/250px-SiemensForum.jpg)](/wiki/File:SiemensForum.jpg)

[Siemens-Forum](/wiki/SiemensForum_M%C3%BCnchen "SiemensForum München") in Munich

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Hypo-Haus.JPG/250px-Hypo-Haus.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hypo-Haus.JPG)

The [HypoVereinsbank](/wiki/HypoVereinsbank "HypoVereinsbank") tower

Munich has the strongest economy of any German city according to a study[[210]](#cite_note-Icm-muenchen.de-213) and the lowest unemployment rate (5.4% in July 2020) of any German city of more than a million people (the others being [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), Hamburg and [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne")).[[211]](#cite_note-214)[[212]](#cite_note-215) Munich [ranks third](/wiki/List_of_German_cities_by_GDP "List of German cities by GDP") on the list of German cities by gross domestic product (GDP). In addition, it is one of the most attractive business locations in Germany.[[210]](#cite_note-Icm-muenchen.de-213) The city is also the economic centre of [southern Germany](/wiki/Southern_Germany "Southern Germany"). Munich topped the ranking of the magazine *Capital* in February 2005 for the economic prospects between 2002 and 2011 in 60 German cities.

Munich is a financial, business and commercial center and [global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city") that holds the headquarters of many companies. This includes more companies listed by the [DAX](/wiki/DAX "DAX") than any other German city, as well as the German or European headquarters of many foreign companies such as [McDonald's](/wiki/McDonald%27s "McDonald's") and [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"). One of the best-known newly established Munich companies is [Flixbus](/wiki/Flixbus "Flixbus").

### Manufacturing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=76 "Edit section: Manufacturing")]

Munich holds the headquarters of [Siemens AG](/wiki/Siemens "Siemens") (electronics), [BMW](/wiki/BMW "BMW") (car), [Traton](/wiki/Traton "Traton") (truck manufacturer, engineering), [MTU Aero Engines](/wiki/MTU_Aero_Engines "MTU Aero Engines") (aircraft engine manufacturer), [Linde](/wiki/The_Linde_Group "The Linde Group") (gases) and [Rohde & Schwarz](/wiki/Rohde_%26_Schwarz "Rohde & Schwarz") ([electronics](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics")). Among German cities with more than 500,000 inhabitants, purchasing power is highest in Munich (€26,648 per inhabitant) as of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit).[[213]](#cite_note-216) In 2006, Munich blue-collar workers enjoyed an average hourly wage of €18.62 (ca. $20).[[214]](#cite_note-217)

The breakdown by cities proper (not metropolitan areas) of Global 500 cities listed Munich in 8th position in 2009.[[215]](#cite_note-218) Munich is also a centre for [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), software and other [service industries](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy"). Furthermore, Munich is the home of the headquarters of many other large companies such as the [injection moulding machine](/wiki/Injection_moulding_machine "Injection moulding machine") manufacturer [Krauss-Maffei](/wiki/Krauss-Maffei "Krauss-Maffei"), and its arms manufacturing branch [Krauss-Maffei & Wegmann](/wiki/Krauss-Maffei_Wegmann "Krauss-Maffei Wegmann"), the camera and lighting manufacturer [Arri](/wiki/Arri "Arri"), the semiconductor firm [Infineon Technologies](/wiki/Infineon_Technologies "Infineon Technologies") (headquartered in the suburban town of [Neubiberg](/wiki/Neubiberg "Neubiberg")), lighting giant [Osram](/wiki/Osram "Osram"), as well as the German or European headquarters of many foreign companies such as [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft").

### Finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=77 "Edit section: Finance")]

Munich has significance as a [financial centre](/wiki/Munich%27s_financial_community "Munich's financial community") (second only to [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt_am_Main "Frankfurt am Main")), being home of [HypoVereinsbank](/wiki/HypoVereinsbank "HypoVereinsbank") and the [Bayerische Landesbank](/wiki/Bayerische_Landesbank "Bayerische Landesbank"). It outranks [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt_am_Main "Frankfurt am Main") though as home of insurance companies such as [Allianz](/wiki/Allianz "Allianz") (insurance) and [Munich Re](/wiki/Munich_Re "Munich Re") ([re-insurance](/wiki/Reinsurance "Reinsurance")).[[216]](#cite_note-219)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=78 "Edit section: Media")]

Munich is the largest publishing city in Europe[[217]](#cite_note-220) and home to the *[Süddeutsche Zeitung](/wiki/S%C3%BCddeutsche_Zeitung "Süddeutsche Zeitung")*, one of Germany's biggest daily newspapers. The city is also the location of the programming headquarters of Germany's largest public broadcasting network, [ARD](/wiki/ARD_(broadcaster) "ARD (broadcaster)"), while the largest commercial network, [Pro7-Sat1 Media AG](/wiki/ProSieben "ProSieben"), is headquartered in the suburb of [Unterföhring](/wiki/Unterf%C3%B6hring "Unterföhring"). The headquarters of the German branch of [Random House](/wiki/Random_House "Random House"), the world's largest publishing house, and of [Burda publishing group](/wiki/Hubert_Burda_Media "Hubert Burda Media") are also in Munich.

The [Bavaria Film Studios](/wiki/Bavaria_Film_Studios "Bavaria Film Studios") are located in the suburb of [Grünwald](/wiki/Gr%C3%BCnwald,_Bavaria "Grünwald, Bavaria"). They are one of Europe's biggest film production studios.[[218]](#cite_note-221)
