## Quality of life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=80 "Edit section: Quality of life")]

Most Munich residents enjoy a high quality of life. [Mercer HR Consulting](/wiki/Mercer_(consulting_firm) "Mercer (consulting firm)") consistently rates the city among the top 10 cities with the highest quality of life worldwide – a 2011 survey ranked Munich as 4th.[[219]](#cite_note-222) In 2007 the same company also ranked Munich as the 39th most expensive in the world and most expensive major city in Germany.[[220]](#cite_note-223) Munich enjoys a thriving economy, driven by the information technology, biotechnology, and publishing sectors. Environmental pollution is low, although as of 2006[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit) the city council is concerned about levels of [particulate matter](/wiki/Particulate_matter "Particulate matter") (PM), especially along the city's major thoroughfares. Since the enactment of [EU legislation concerning the concentration of particulate](/wiki/Particulate#EU_legislation "Particulate") in the air, environmental groups such as [Greenpeace](/wiki/Greenpeace "Greenpeace") have staged large protest rallies to urge the city council and the state government to take a harder stance on pollution.[[221]](#cite_note-224) Due to the high standard of living in and the thriving economy of the city and the region, there was an influx of people and Munich's population surpassed 1.5 million by June 2015, an increase of more than 20% in 10 years.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=81 "Edit section: Transport")]

Munich has an extensive public transport system consisting of an underground metro, trams, buses and high-speed rail. In 2015, the transport [modal share](/wiki/Modal_share "Modal share") in Munich was 38 percent public transport, 25 percent car, 23 percent walking, and 15 percent bicycle.[[222]](#cite_note-www.mvg.de-225) Its public transport system delivered 566 million passenger trips that year.[[223]](#cite_note-226)

Munich is the hub of a developed regional transportation system, including the second-largest airport in Germany and the [Berlin–Munich high-speed railway](/wiki/Berlin%E2%80%93Munich_high-speed_railway "Berlin–Munich high-speed railway"), which connects Munich to the German capital city with a journey time of about 4 hours. [Flixmobility](/wiki/Flixmobility "Flixmobility") which offers intercity coach service is headquartered in Munich.

The trade fair [Transport Logistic](/wiki/Transport_Logistic "Transport Logistic") is held every two years at the *Neue Messe München* (Messe München International).

### Public transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=82 "Edit section: Public transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Verkehrsnetz_M%C3%BCnchen.png/500px-Verkehrsnetz_M%C3%BCnchen.png)](/wiki/File:Verkehrsnetz_M%C3%BCnchen.png)

Public transport network

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/MVG_2168_Munchen_Ost_18-08-2007.jpg/250px-MVG_2168_Munchen_Ost_18-08-2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:MVG_2168_Munchen_Ost_18-08-2007.jpg)

A class R2 *Straßenbahn* (*Tram*) on route 19 at Ostbahnhof

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/S-bahn-muenchen.jpg/250px-S-bahn-muenchen.jpg)](/wiki/File:S-bahn-muenchen.jpg)

[Munich's S-Bahn](/wiki/Munich_S-Bahn "Munich S-Bahn") at the [Marienplatz station](/wiki/Munich_Marienplatz_station "Munich Marienplatz station")

For its urban population of 2.6 million people, Munich and its closest suburbs have a comprehensive network of public transport incorporating the [Munich U-Bahn](/wiki/Munich_U-Bahn "Munich U-Bahn"), the [Munich S-Bahn](/wiki/Munich_S-Bahn "Munich S-Bahn"), trams and buses. The system is supervised by the [Munich Transport and Tariff Association](/wiki/Munich_Transport_and_Tariff_Association "Munich Transport and Tariff Association") (*Münchner Verkehrs- und Tarifverbund*). The [Munich tramway](/wiki/Munich_tramway "Munich tramway") is the oldest existing public transportation system in the city, which has been in operation since 1876. Munich also has an extensive network of bus lines. The average amount of time people spend commuting to and from work with public transit in Munich on a weekday is 56 min.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The extensive network of subway and tram lines assists and complement pedestrian movement in the city centre. The 700m-long Kaufinger Straße, which starts near the Main train station, forms a pedestrian east–west spine that traverses almost the entire centre. Major spines and many smaller streets cover an extensive area of the centre that can be enjoyed on foot and bike. These attributes result from applying the principle of [filtered permeability](/wiki/Permeability_(spatial_and_transport_planning) "Permeability (spatial and transport planning)"). Pedestrian and bike paths, which permeate the entire Munich city centre, go through public squares and open spaces for enjoyment. Munich city centre was subject to [urban planning](/wiki/Urban_planning "Urban planning") and has a comprehensive model for laying out neighborhoods and districts according to [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan").[[224]](#cite_note-227)

### Cycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=83 "Edit section: Cycling")]

Main article: [Cycling in Munich](/wiki/Cycling_in_Munich "Cycling in Munich")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Fahrradschild-bjs210429-02.jpg/250px-Fahrradschild-bjs210429-02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fahrradschild-bjs210429-02.jpg)

Map of Munich's cycling network

Cycling has a strong presence in the city and is recognized as a good alternative. The growing number of [bicycle lanes](/wiki/Segregated_cycle_facilities "Segregated cycle facilities") are widely used throughout the year. Cycle paths can be found alongside the majority of sidewalks and streets, although the newer or renovated ones are much easier to tell apart from pavements than older ones. A modern [bike hire system](/wiki/Call_a_Bike "Call a Bike") is available within the area bounded by the *Mittlerer Ring*.

### Cultural history trails and bicycle routes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=84 "Edit section: Cultural history trails and bicycle routes")]

Since 2001, historically interesting places in Munich can be explored via the [List of cultural history trails in Munich](/wiki/List_of_cultural_history_trails_in_Munich "List of cultural history trails in Munich") (*KulturGeschichtsPfade*). Sign-posted cycle routes are the Outer *Äußere Radlring* (outer cycle route) and the *RadlRing München*.[[225]](#cite_note-228)

### Munich Central Train Station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=85 "Edit section: Munich Central Train Station")]

Main article: [München Hauptbahnhof](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchen_Hauptbahnhof "München Hauptbahnhof")

Munich Central Train Station (*München Hauptbahnhof*) is the central railway station located in the city centre and is the long-distance station in Munich.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[226]](#cite_note-229)

Munich Central Train Station serves about 450,000 passengers a day, which puts it on par with other large stations in Germany. Munich Central Train Station alongside [München Ost railway station](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchen_Ost_railway_station "München Ost railway station") are two of the 21 stations in Germany classified by [Deutsche Bahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bahn "Deutsche Bahn") as a [category 1 station](/wiki/German_railway_station_categories "German railway station categories").

The central mainline station is a [terminal station](/wiki/Terminal_station "Terminal station") with 32 [platforms](/wiki/Railway_platform "Railway platform"). The subterranean [S-Bahn](/wiki/Munich_S-Bahn "Munich S-Bahn") with 2 platforms and [U-Bahn](/wiki/Munich_U-Bahn "Munich U-Bahn") stations with 6 platforms are through stations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Intercity-Express](/wiki/Intercity-Express "Intercity-Express") (ICE) and [EuroCity-Express](/wiki/EuroCity-Express "EuroCity-Express") (ECE; Munich–[Bregenz](/wiki/Bregenz "Bregenz")–[Zurich](/wiki/Zurich "Zurich")) trains – and starting in 2026 also [TGV](/wiki/TGV "TGV") trains (Munich–[Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"))[[227]](#cite_note-230) – stop at Munich Central Train Station. [InterCity](/wiki/InterCity "InterCity") (IC) and [EuroCity](/wiki/EuroCity "EuroCity") (EC) trains to destinations east of Munich also stop at the München Ost railway station. Munich is connected to [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg") via [Ingolstadt](/wiki/Ingolstadt "Ingolstadt") by the [Nuremberg–Munich high-speed railway](/wiki/Nuremberg%E2%80%93Munich_high-speed_railway "Nuremberg–Munich high-speed railway") and [Berlin–Munich high-speed railway](/wiki/Berlin%E2%80%93Munich_high-speed_railway "Berlin–Munich high-speed railway").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The old air raid shelter next to platform 11 of Munich Central Train Station was an important distribution point for [guest workers](/wiki/Guest_worker "Guest worker") (*Gastarbeiter*) between 1960 and 1973. At peak more than 1,000 guest workers arrived per day, in total 1.8 million guest workers passed through Munich Central Train Station.[[228]](#cite_note-231)

### Autobahns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=86 "Edit section: Autobahns")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Karte_Fernstra%C3%9Fen_M%C3%BCnchen.png/250px-Karte_Fernstra%C3%9Fen_M%C3%BCnchen.png)](/wiki/File:Karte_Fernstra%C3%9Fen_M%C3%BCnchen.png)

Munich [motorway](/wiki/Autobahn "Autobahn") network

Munich is an integral part of the [Autobahn](/wiki/Autobahn "Autobahn") network of southern Germany. Motorways from [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart") (W), [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg"), [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt_am_Main "Frankfurt am Main") and Berlin (N), [Deggendorf](/wiki/Deggendorf "Deggendorf") and [Passau](/wiki/Passau "Passau") (E), [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg") and [Innsbruck](/wiki/Innsbruck "Innsbruck") (SE), [Garmisch Partenkirchen](/wiki/Garmisch-Partenkirchen "Garmisch-Partenkirchen") (S) and [Lindau](/wiki/Lindau "Lindau") (SW) terminate at Munich, allowing direct access to the different parts of Germany, Austria and Italy.

Traffic is often very heavy in and around Munich. [Traffic congestion](/wiki/Traffic_congestion "Traffic congestion") are commonplace at the beginning and end of major Bavarian holidays. There are few "green waves" or [roundabouts](/wiki/Roundabout "Roundabout"), and an abundance of construction sites.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Munich has introduced an [environmental zone](/wiki/Traffic_and_Environmental_Zone "Traffic and Environmental Zone") and was among the first German cities to require a [green sticker](/wiki/Green_sticker "Green sticker") for vehicles, these are a requirement when entering the city or driving in the wider surrounding area.[[229]](#cite_note-232)

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=87 "Edit section: Air")]

#### Munich International Airport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=88 "Edit section: Munich International Airport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Flughafen_turm.png/250px-Flughafen_turm.png)](/wiki/File:Flughafen_turm.png)

[Munich International Airport](/wiki/Munich_Airport "Munich Airport") (MUC)

[Franz Josef Strauss International Airport](/wiki/Munich_Airport "Munich Airport") ([IATA](/wiki/International_Air_Transport_Association "International Air Transport Association"): MUC, [ICAO](/wiki/International_Civil_Aviation_Organization "International Civil Aviation Organization"): EDDM) is the second-largest airport in Germany and seventh-largest in Europe after [London Heathrow](/wiki/London_Heathrow_Airport "London Heathrow Airport"), [Paris Charles de Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle_Airport "Charles de Gaulle Airport"), [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt_International_Airport "Frankfurt International Airport"), [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam_Airport_Schiphol "Amsterdam Airport Schiphol"), [Madrid](/wiki/Barajas_Airport "Barajas Airport") and [Istanbul Atatürk](/wiki/Atat%C3%BCrk_International_Airport "Atatürk International Airport"). It is used by about 46 million passengers a year, and lies some 30 km (19 mi) north east of the city centre. It replaced the smaller [Munich-Riem Airport](/wiki/Munich-Riem_Airport "Munich-Riem Airport") in 1992. The airport can be reached by suburban train lines from the city. From the [main railway station](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchen_Hauptbahnhof "München Hauptbahnhof") the journey takes 40–45 minutes. A [magnetic levitation train](/wiki/Maglev_(transport) "Maglev (transport)") (called [Transrapid](/wiki/Transrapid "Transrapid")), which was to have run at speeds of up to 400 km/h (249 mph) from the central station to the airport in a travel time of 10 minutes, had been approved,[[230]](#cite_note-233) but was cancelled in March 2008 because of cost escalation and after heavy protests.[[231]](#cite_note-234) [Lufthansa](/wiki/Lufthansa "Lufthansa") opened its second hub at the airport when Terminal 2 was opened in 2003.

#### Other airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=89 "Edit section: Other airports")]

In 2008, the Bavarian state government granted a licence to expand Oberpfaffenhofen Air Station located west of Munich, for commercial use. These plans were opposed by many residents in the Oberpfaffenhofen area as well as other branches of local government, including the city of Munich, which took the case to court.[[232]](#cite_note-235) However, in October 2009, the permit allowing up to 9725 business flights per year to depart from or land at Oberpfaffenhofen was confirmed by a regional judge.[[233]](#cite_note-236)

Despite being 110 km (68 mi) from Munich, [Memmingen Airport](/wiki/Memmingen_Airport "Memmingen Airport") has been advertised as Airport Munich West. After 2005, passenger traffic of nearby [Augsburg Airport](/wiki/Augsburg_Airport "Augsburg Airport") was relocated to Munich Airport, leaving the Augsburg region of Bavaria without an air passenger airport within close reach.

## Around Munich

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=90 "Edit section: Around Munich")]

### Nearby towns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=91 "Edit section: Nearby towns")]

The Munich agglomeration sprawls across the plain of the [Alpine foothills](/wiki/Foothills "Foothills") comprising about 2.6 million inhabitants. Several smaller traditional Bavarian towns and cities like [Dachau](/wiki/Dachau,_Bavaria "Dachau, Bavaria"), [Freising](/wiki/Freising "Freising"), [Erding](/wiki/Erding "Erding"), [Starnberg](/wiki/Starnberg "Starnberg"), [Landshut](/wiki/Landshut "Landshut") and [Moosburg](/wiki/Moosburg "Moosburg") are today part of the Greater Munich Region, formed by Munich and the surrounding districts, making up the [Munich Metropolitan Region](/wiki/Munich_Metropolitan_Region "Munich Metropolitan Region"), which has a population of about 6 million people.[[4]](#cite_note-mmr_official_site-6)

### Recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Munich&action=edit&section=92 "Edit section: Recreation")]

South of Munich, there are numerous nearby freshwater lakes such as [Lake Starnberg](/wiki/Lake_Starnberg "Lake Starnberg"), [Ammersee](/wiki/Ammersee "Ammersee"), [Chiemsee](/wiki/Chiemsee "Chiemsee"), [Walchensee](/wiki/Walchensee "Walchensee"), [Kochelsee](/wiki/Kochelsee "Kochelsee"), [Tegernsee](/wiki/Tegernsee_(lake) "Tegernsee (lake)"), [Schliersee](/wiki/Schliersee_(lake) "Schliersee (lake)"), [Simssee](/wiki/Simssee "Simssee"), [Staffelsee](/wiki/Staffelsee "Staffelsee"), [Wörthsee](/wiki/W%C3%B6rthsee "Wörthsee"), [Kirchsee](/wiki/Kirchsee_(Bavaria) "Kirchsee (Bavaria)") and the [Osterseen](/wiki/Osterseen "Osterseen") (Easter Lakes), which are popular among Munich residents for recreation, swimming and watersports and can be quickly reached by car and a few also by Munich's [S-Bahn](/wiki/Munich_S-Bahn "Munich S-Bahn").[[234]](#cite_note-237)
